title: Capitellar Osteochondritis Dissecans — Stable vs Unstable

title: Distal Biceps Tendon Tear (Retraction + Lacertus)

title: Distal Biceps Tendon Tear (Retraction + Lacertus)

title: Medial Epicondylitis with Adjacent Ulnar Neuritis
textPreview: The MEDIAL TRIAD: medial epicondylitis (golfer's elbow), the anterior-bundle UCL, and the ulnar nerve travel together because the cubital tunnel sits immediately posterior to the flexor-pronator origin. When you call medial epicondylitis, do not stop - always interrogate the u...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Medial Epicondylitis with Adjacent Ulnar Neuritis
textPreview: Anchor the ulnar-neuritis call to STRUCTURE, not signal alone: mild ulnar-nerve T2 hyperintensity occurs in asymptomatic elbows. Make the diagnosis on caliber enlargement + muscle denervation (FCU/ulnar FDP/ulnar intrinsics) + clinical correlation, using the ipsilateral median...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Medial Epicondylitis with Adjacent Ulnar Neuritis
textPreview: Management/disposition pearl: uncomplicated medial epicondylitis with ulnar neuritis is REHAB-first (swing/activity modification, flexor-pronator and kinetic-chain rehab, nerve hygiene, night extension splinting). The presence of ulnar symptoms changes the OPERATIVE plan if su...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Medial Epicondylitis with Adjacent Ulnar Neuritis
textPreview: The single most operative-changing nerve finding is DYNAMIC SUBLUXATION: an ulnar nerve that subluxates over the medial epicondyle in flexion (or a coexisting anconeus epitrochlearis or snapping triceps) favors selective ANTERIOR TRANSPOSITION over in-situ decompression. Asses...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Cubital Tunnel Syndrome — Ulnar Neuritis ± Subluxation
textPreview: Cubital tunnel syndrome is the SECOND most common compression neuropathy after carpal tunnel — and the most common at the elbow. The MRI job is not just to confirm an enlarged, T2-bright nerve, but to define the STRUCTURAL cause (accessory muscle, ganglion, osteophyte) and the...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Cubital Tunnel Syndrome — Ulnar Neuritis ± Subluxation
textPreview: Management/disposition pearl: the finding that changes the operation is a SUBLUXATING nerve, an accessory muscle (anconeus epitrochlearis), or a co-existing snapping triceps — any of these favors anterior TRANSPOSITION (and the triceps must be addressed if it snaps) over simpl...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Cubital Tunnel Syndrome — Ulnar Neuritis ± Subluxation
textPreview: Snapping triceps is the don't-miss companion: the medial head of the triceps can subluxate over the medial epicondyle in flexion together with the ulnar nerve. If you decompress or transpose the nerve but ignore a snapping triceps, the patient keeps snapping — so interrogate t...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Cubital Tunnel Syndrome — Ulnar Neuritis ± Subluxation
textPreview: Anchor the diagnosis to CALIBER change plus muscle DENERVATION plus clinical correlation — not signal alone. Mild ulnar-nerve T2 hyperintensity occurs in asymptomatic elbows, so compare the nerve to the ipsilateral MEDIAN nerve (internal control) and the CONTRALATERAL ulnar ne...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Valgus Extension Overload — Posteromedial Loose Body
textPreview: Valgus extension overload is a CHAIN, not a single lesion: repetitive throwing valgus overloads the UCL, and the resulting medial laxity lets the posteromedial olecranon impact the olecranon-fossa wall in terminal extension, producing the posteromedial osteophyte, kissing chon...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Valgus Extension Overload — Posteromedial Loose Body
textPreview: The management-changing pearl: the loose body and osteophyte cause the catching, but the UCL is the keystone. Debriding the osteophyte or removing the loose body WITHOUT addressing UCL insufficiency can UNMASK or WORSEN valgus instability - and over-resecting the posteromedial...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Valgus Extension Overload — Posteromedial Loose Body

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Valgus Extension Overload — Posteromedial Loose Body
textPreview: Complete the medial triad in every thrower: UCL + flexor-pronator origin + ulnar nerve travel together. Document the ulnar neuritis and any flexor-pronator tendinosis, but anchor the ulnar-nerve call to caliber change plus denervation plus clinical correlation, not mild T2 sig...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Occult Radial Head Fracture vs Normal Variant (Pseudodefect)
textPreview: A displaced posterior fat-pad sign with a normal radiograph is an OCCULT INTRA-ARTICULAR FRACTURE until proven otherwise — in an adult after a FOOSH, the radial head is the most common culprit. Do not 'reassure away' an effusion just because no line is visible on plain film; i...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Occult Radial Head Fracture vs Normal Variant (Pseudodefect)
textPreview: MRI earns its keep by distinguishing a TRUE FRACTURE (a discrete low-signal LINE plus marrow edema) from a BONE BRUISE (marrow edema WITHOUT a line). That distinction changes immobilization, follow-up, and counseling on healing time.

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Occult Radial Head Fracture vs Normal Variant (Pseudodefect)
textPreview: The don't-overcall counterpoint: the capitellar PSEUDODEFECT (posterolateral bare-area groove) and the transverse TROCHLEAR RIDGE are normal variants — calling either an osteochondral lesion or fracture sends a patient down an unnecessary surgical pathway. Capitellar OCD requi...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Occult Radial Head Fracture vs Normal Variant (Pseudodefect)
textPreview: The radial-head SURGICAL TRIGGER is functional, not a single number: a mechanical BLOCK to forearm rotation, an articular fragment displaced enough to impede motion, or comminution of the whole head (Mason III). The '2 mm' figure alone does not mandate surgery — many minimally...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Occult Radial Head Fracture vs Normal Variant (Pseudodefect)
textPreview: Run the radiocapitellar line on every projection/plane (it must bisect the capitellum) to avoid missing an occult Monteggia, and in CHILDREN convert the same fat-pad sign into a dedicated hunt for the non-union-prone lateral condyle (Salter-Harris IV) fracture — a classic pedi...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: Edema is the smoke — find the fire: tie every elbow marrow edema to a fracture line, a physis, a subchondral cyst, or an osteochondral fragment before moving on.

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: Capitellar OCD instability is about the interface: a fluid line undercutting the fragment, a sizeable/multiple cyst, a cartilage breach, or a displaced fragment = unstable. Surrounding edema alone is not, and an isolated high-T2 line is indeterminate.

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: A displaced posterior fat pad after adult elbow trauma is a fracture until disproven — radial head first; the surgical trigger is a block to forearm rotation, not the 2 mm number alone.

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: Medial elbow pain is a triad: read the flexor-pronator origin, the UCL to the sublime tubercle, AND the ulnar nerve together — they injure together in valgus overload.

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: Read lateral epicondylitis? Trace the LUCL to the supinator crest. Read medial epicondylitis? Trace the UCL. The adjacent ligament is where the surgery hides.

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: Localize a nerve by its muscle: isolated pronator quadratus edema = AIN (consider Parsonage–Turner, not surgery); extensor compartment = PIN at the arcade of Frohse; FCU / ulnar-FDP / intrinsics = ulnar at the cubital tunnel — and compare the ulnar nerve to the median nerve an...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: In the post-dislocation elbow the LCL is usually the primary lesion (O'Driscoll lateral-to-medial circle); chase the coronoid too — a small anteromedial facet fracture means varus posteromedial rotatory instability and is surgical.

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Reflexively ordering MR arthrography for every elbow, or treating an elbow 'ABER'/valgus-applied sequence as a validated routine like the shoulder
textPreview: Non-contrast 3T MRI answers most elbow questions (tendons, OCD, nerves, occult fracture, loose bodies). Add direct MR arthrography specifically when a UCL undersurface (T-sign) tear is suspected but non-contrast is equivocal, or when loose-body count/OCD stability will change ...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Calling lateral epicondylitis (or medial epicondylitis) and stopping the read
textPreview: The symptomatic tendinosis is rarely the management-changing lesion. Trace the LUCL to the supinator crest beneath every common-extensor tendinosis (a deep undersurface tear there means PLRI), and trace the UCL to the sublime tubercle plus interrogate the ulnar nerve beneath e...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Dismissing a small coronoid fragment in a post-dislocation elbow because it looks small on radiographs
textPreview: Small does not mean stable at the coronoid. An anteromedial-facet fracture is highly unstable, implies varus posteromedial rotatory instability, and is surgical; untreated it drives early arthrosis. In any dislocation/terrible-triad elbow, close the O'Driscoll circle — LCL/LUC...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Reading tendons and ligaments first and leaving bones/marrow for the end
textPreview: Read context and bones/marrow as steps 1-2 while attention is freshest. The high-consequence osseous lesions (unstable OCD, olecranon stress fracture, occult radial-head fracture, the anteromedial coronoid facet, a marrow-replacing lesion) are exactly what gets lost to fatigue...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Calling a capitellar OCD unstable on the basis of surrounding marrow edema, or applying a hard 5 mm subchondral-cyst cutoff
textPreview: Surrounding marrow edema is present in stable AND unstable lesions and is not a stability sign; an isolated high-T2 line without a cartilage breach or cyst is INDETERMINATE (it may be healing granulation tissue). Instability is a fluid-bright line completely undercutting the f...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Treating a displaced posterior fat pad with a 'normal' radiograph as no fracture, or sending every Mason II radial-head fracture to surgery on the 2 mm number
textPreview: A visible (displaced) posterior fat pad in an adult after trauma means hemarthrosis from an occult intra-articular fracture — radial head most common — until proven otherwise; MRI shows the low-signal line plus fat-suppressed-bright marrow edema and separates a true fracture f...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Ascribing every elbow loose body to a throwing lesion and clearing a child's 'fat pad without a line'
textPreview: Primary elbow OA (coronoid/olecranon-tip and fossa osteophytes, capitellar/radial-head chondral loss) is the most common source of loose bodies and mechanical symptoms in the non-thrower/laborer. In a child, the lateral condyle fracture (Salter-Harris IV) is the most non-union...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Calling lateral epicondylitis and stopping, without tracing the LUCL to the supinator crest
textPreview: The LUCL/RCL origin lies deep to the common extensor tendon, so a deep undersurface tear that undercuts or communicates with the radiocapitellar joint and involves the LUCL implies PLRI — the lesion that converts refractory 'tennis elbow' into a surgical instability problem an...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Dismissing a small anteromedial coronoid facet fragment as stable because it looks small on radiographs
textPreview: The anteromedial facet fragment is small on radiographs but highly unstable — it implies varus posteromedial rotatory instability (VPMRI), a distinct mechanism from PLRI, and untreated drives early arthrosis. It is surgical. Classify it with the O'Driscoll anteromedial-facet s...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Assuming the medial (ulnar) collateral ligament is the primary lesion in a simple elbow dislocation
textPreview: By the O'Driscoll circle, soft-tissue disruption proceeds lateral-to-medial — LCL/LUCL first, then anterior and posterior capsule, and the MCL last — so the LCL is the primary lesion in most simple dislocations and valgus stability is often preserved. Comment explicitly on LUC...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine

textPreview: Mild intraneural T2 brightness occurs in asymptomatic elbows (the nerve angulates and is closely apposed at the cubital tunnel). Anchor the diagnosis to caliber enlargement at/just distal to the medial epicondyle PLUS target-muscle (FCU, ulnar FDP, ulnar intrinsic) denervation...

textPreview: Denervation has THREE phases: NO MRI change for the first ~2–4 weeks, then SUBACUTE T2/STIR muscle edema (potentially reversible with reinnervation), then CHRONIC T1 fatty atrophy — so the edema phase is subacute, not acute. And an AIN (pure-motor) palsy is frequently neuralgi...

textPreview: In an adult after trauma, a visible (displaced) posterior fat pad means hemarthrosis from an occult intra-articular fracture until proven otherwise — radial head most commonly. Search the marrow on MRI for the line; in children think supracondylar and the non-union-prone later...

title: Radial head
textPreview: Watch for an occult radial-head fracture after a fall — a marrow-edema line with a displaced posterior fat pad; the surgical trigger is a mechanical block to forearm rotation or comminution (Mason III), not the 2 mm number alone.

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Common flexor-pronator origin
textPreview: Watch the medial 'valgus triad' together: the flexor-pronator origin, the UCL to the sublime tubercle, and the ulnar nerve just posterior — valgus overload injures all three.

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Ulnar nerve (cubital tunnel)
textPreview: Watch for a structural cause that can change operative planning — dynamic flexion subluxation of the nerve, an anconeus epitrochlearis, or a snapping triceps may favor transposition over in-situ decompression.

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Coronoid process
textPreview: Watch the anteromedial coronoid facet — a small-looking fracture can imply varus posteromedial rotatory instability and should trigger surgical review; in any post-dislocation elbow, also comment on the LUCL (the usual primary lesion).

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Olecranon & triceps insertion
textPreview: Watch the posteromedial olecranon in the thrower for a valgus-extension-overload osteophyte and a posterior loose body — and remember debriding the osteophyte without addressing UCL laxity can unmask valgus instability.

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Trochlea / capitellum (articular surface)
textPreview: Watch for a posterolateral radiocapitellar plica: a thin fold can be normal, but thickened/edematous plica with snapping/catching can mimic a loose body or OCD symptoms.

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Fat pads (coronoid & olecranon fossae)
textPreview: A visible posterior fat pad after adult elbow trauma is a fracture until proven otherwise — radial head first.

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Ulnar collateral ligament (anterior bundle)
textPreview: variant: A small synovial recess at the distal UCL is normal — the T-sign threshold is fluid tracking BEYOND the articular-cartilage edge, not any sliver of fluid at the attachment, and it should be interpreted with symptoms/laxity rather than as an automatic surgical indicati...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Radial head
textPreview: measure: Mason framework: I = non-/minimally displaced, II = displaced (commonly >2 mm) partial-articular, III = comminuted — but the surgical trigger is a mechanical block to rotation, not the 2 mm number alone.

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Ulnar nerve (cubital tunnel)
textPreview: variant: Mild ulnar-nerve T2 hyperintensity occurs in asymptomatic elbows; there is no universal cross-sectional-area cutoff — compare to the median nerve and the contralateral side.

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Trochlea / capitellum (articular surface)
textPreview: variant: A thin low-signal posterolateral radiocapitellar synovial plica/fold may be normal. It should not be mistaken for a loose body or an unstable OCD fragment unless it is thickened/edematous, trapped in the joint, or matches mechanical symptoms. | measure: When symptoms ...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Brachialis & distal biceps (anterior)
textPreview: variant: An intact lacertus fibrosus can leave the stump near the radial neck despite a full rupture — report complete-vs-partial AND lacertus integrity, not just the retraction distance.

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Radial head
textPreview: The radial head is the round disc of the proximal radius articulating with the capitellum laterally — the most common site of an occult fracture after a fall on the outstretched hand. Confirm the cortex is smooth and the marrow uniform; the posterolateral capitellar pseudodefe...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Common flexor-pronator origin
textPreview: The common flexor-pronator tendon arises off the medial epicondyle and overlies the UCL. It is the site of medial epicondylitis (golfer's elbow) — read it together with the UCL and the ulnar nerve (the medial 'valgus triad'), since valgus overload injures all three. | Common f...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Get oriented
textPreview: Axial T2 FS cross-section through the elbow: the bone is central, ringed by the flexor-pronator (medial), extensor (lateral), brachialis (anterior), and triceps (posterior) compartments. This is the plane for the nerves — the ulnar nerve in the cubital tunnel, the radial nerve...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Ulnar nerve (cubital tunnel)
textPreview: The ulnar nerve sits in the cubital tunnel posterior to the medial epicondyle, beneath Osborne's retinaculum. Normal = an ovoid nerve of caliber and signal similar to the median nerve and the contralateral side. Assess for flexion subluxation, an anconeus epitrochlearis, or a ...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Common flexor-pronator (cross-section)
textPreview: The common flexor-pronator mass arises off the medial epicondyle anteromedially. In cross-section, grade tendinosis vs a surface-reaching tear at the origin; the ulnar nerve sits just posterior to it, which is why golfer's elbow and ulnar neuritis travel together. | Common fle...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Common extensor (cross-section)
textPreview: The common extensor origin arises off the lateral epicondyle anterolaterally. Cross-section helps grade extensor tendinosis vs a deep tear; the radial nerve and its PIN branch run nearby at the radiocapitellar level (the PIN dives under the arcade of Frohse into the supinator)...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Brachialis
textPreview: The brachialis is the broad anterior muscle that inserts on the coronoid/ulnar tuberosity — the primary elbow flexor, lying behind the biceps. The distal biceps tendon runs through the antecubital fossa toward its radial-tuberosity footprint; a dedicated FABS view can help whe...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Triceps
textPreview: The triceps occupies the posterior compartment and inserts broadly on the olecranon. Its deep central fibers can tear first while the superficial expansion stays intact — so a triceps tear can look partial despite significant extension weakness. Watch for a medial triceps head...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Do-not-overcall checkpoint
textPreview: On axial elbow MRI, do not diagnose cubital tunnel syndrome from a bright ulnar nerve alone. Compare nerve size and signal with the median nerve and the other side, then look for denervation or a structural cause such as subluxation, an accessory anconeus epitrochlearis, a gan...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Coronoid process
textPreview: The coronoid is the anterior beak of the proximal ulna and the keystone of post-traumatic stability — an anteromedial facet fracture can look small on radiographs yet imply varus posteromedial rotatory instability, so it deserves instability-focused surgical review. The sublim...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Olecranon & triceps insertion
textPreview: The olecranon is the posterior beak of the ulna where the triceps inserts. In the thrower, the posteromedial olecranon is the site of the valgus-extension-overload osteophyte and a posterior loose body. Sweep the olecranon fossa just above it for loose bodies on this fluid-sen...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Fat pads (coronoid & olecranon fossae)
textPreview: The anterior fat pad sits in the coronoid fossa and is normally visible; the posterior fat pad hides in the olecranon fossa and is normally NOT seen. After trauma, an elevated anterior fat pad or any visible posterior fat pad signals an effusion/hemarthrosis from an occult fra...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
itemId: Complete distal biceps avulsion (or a complete tear with an intact lacertus that doesn't retract)
title: Complete distal biceps avulsion (or a complete tear with an intact lacertus that doesn't retract)
textPreview: A time-sensitive surgical repair (≈40–50% supination / ≈30% flexion strength lost). An intact lacertus tethers the tendon so it neither retracts nor shows an obvious clinical gap — the classic undercall as 'partial.' Report partial-vs-complete, retraction in cm, and lacertus i...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Unstable capitellar OCD
textPreview: A fluid line undercutting the fragment, sizeable/multiple subchondral cysts, a cartilage breach, or a displaced fragment/loose body in a young thrower → stop throwing and refer for surgery. Surrounding edema alone is not instability; an isolated high-T2 line is indeterminate.

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
itemId: Anteromedial coronoid facet fracture (and the terrible triad)
title: Anteromedial coronoid facet fracture (and the terrible triad)
textPreview: The small-looking anteromedial facet fragment implies varus posteromedial rotatory instability and is surgical; the terrible triad (dislocation + radial head + coronoid) is unstable with the coronoid and LCL as keystones. Classify the coronoid, look for the Osborne–Cotterill l...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Planes — what each one answers
textPreview: label: Axial | detail: Cross-sections the four compartments. Best for the ulnar nerve in the cubital tunnel (and dynamic flexion subluxation), the radial nerve/PIN at the radiocapitellar level, the common flexor/extensor origins, the annular ligament around the radial neck, an...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Sequences — what they're for
textPreview: label: Fluid-sensitive fat-sat (T2 FS / STIR) | detail: Edema, partial/complete tendon and ligament tears, the OCD fluid rim, marrow injury, effusions, and subacute muscle denervation edema. A finding is more convincing when fluid-bright signal points to it. | label: T1-weight...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Medial compartment
textPreview: label: UCL (ulnar collateral) complex | detail: The anterior bundle is the primary valgus restraint (origin anteroinferior medial epicondyle → insertion at the sublime tubercle of the coronoid); the posterior bundle (cubital tunnel floor) and transverse/Cooper ligament are sec...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Lateral compartment
textPreview: label: Common extensor origin (ECRB) | detail: Off the lateral epicondyle; the site of lateral epicondylitis (angiofibroblastic tendinosis). | label: RCL / LUCL complex | detail: The radial collateral ligament blends into the annular ligament; the lateral ulnar collateral liga...

textPreview: label: Capitellar pseudodefect & trochlear groove | detail: The posterolateral capitellar bare-area groove and the transverse trochlear ridge/groove are normal — not osteochondral lesions. | label: Distal-UCL synovial recess | detail: A small normal synovial recess sits near t...

textPreview: label: Stable (rest + follow-up) | detail: No fluid-bright surrounding signal, intact overlying cartilage, an in-situ fragment, and a low-signal demarcating margin → throwing rest and serial follow-up. | label: Unstable — any of (surgical referral) | detail: A T2 fluid-bright ...

title: Triceps tear
textPreview: label: Tendinosis vs partial vs complete | detail: The deep central fibers fail first while the superficial expansion stays intact — so it can look partial despite near-complete clinical weakness. Complete = a full-thickness gap with a retracted stump ± an olecranon flake. Rep...

courseTitle: Elbow MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Epicondylitis & the adjacent ligament
textPreview: label: Grading | detail: Tendinosis (thickening + intermediate signal, intact fibers, no surface-reaching fluid/gap) → partial-thickness tear → full-thickness gap at the origin. Lateral = common extensor (ECRB); medial = common flexor/pronator. | label: The companion to always...

title: LCL complex, PLRI & the coronoid (post-trauma instability)
textPreview: label: LUCL & PLRI | detail: LUCL: lateral epicondyle → posterior to the radial head → supinator crest; the primary restraint to posterolateral rotatory instability. Trace it on coronal + axial (partial-volume averaging causes misses). There is no standardized single MRI measu...

textPreview: label: What to report | detail: Nerve enlargement and T2 hyperintensity at/just distal to the medial epicondyle, plus denervation of FCU, the ulnar FDP, and the ulnar intrinsics. Compare caliber/signal to the median nerve and the contralateral side (no universal cross-sectiona...

textPreview: label: Non-contrast MRI | detail: The first study for most questions: tendons, OCD, nerves, occult fracture, loose bodies. Non-contrast 3T answers most cases. | label: MR arthrography | detail: Add it for a suspected UCL undersurface (T-sign) tear and for loose-body/chondral-d...

textPreview: Sweep the radial head, capitellum, trochlea, olecranon, and coronoid first — T1 for marrow replacement, fluid-sensitive for edema and fracture lines. Tie every edema signal to a line, physis, cyst, or fragment. | Radial head, capitellum, trochlea, olecranon, coronoid: marrow e...

textPreview: Read the three nerves by caliber + signal against an internal control, and let muscle denervation be the localizing secondary sign. Anchor the diagnosis to a structural change, not signal alone. | Ulnar nerve in the cubital tunnel: caliber/signal vs the contralateral side and ...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Pincer-Type FAI with Acetabular Retroversion/Over-Coverage and Contrecoup Posteroinferior Chondral Injury
textPreview: A 34-year-old female recreational dancer/yoga instructor reports anterior groin pain at the extremes of hip flexion and external rotation, with mechanical catching. FADIR is positive; radiographs show a crossover sign and a prominent ischial spine. The clinical question is FAI...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Cam-Type FAI with Anterosuperior Labral Tear and Rim Chondral Injury
textPreview: The LABRAL tear gets the headlines, but it is the acetabular RIM chondral injury (delamination/full-thickness loss) that most drives surgical weighting and prognosis. Always report chondral status explicitly.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Cam-Type FAI with Anterosuperior Labral Tear and Rim Chondral Injury
textPreview: Pitfall: cam morphology is common in asymptomatic athletes (especially hockey, football, soccer). Imaging morphology alone does NOT equal a surgical lesion - it must concord with FADIR-positive mechanical symptoms.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Cam-Type FAI with Anterosuperior Labral Tear and Rim Chondral Injury
textPreview: Management/disposition pearl: symptomatic cam FAI is NOT an automatic operation. First-line is a structured rehab program (hip and core strengthening, activity modification); hip-preservation (orthopedic) referral for arthroscopic osteochondroplasty and labral repair is for fa...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Cam-Type FAI with Anterosuperior Labral Tear and Rim Chondral Injury
textPreview: An intra-articular diagnostic anesthetic injection that relieves pain confirms an intra-articular source and supports surgical candidacy when the clinical picture is mixed (e.g., concurrent lateral or pubic complaints).

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Tension-Side (Superolateral) Femoral Neck Stress Fracture - Surgical Urgency
textPreview: Location dictates everything: TENSION-side fractures are SUPEROLATERAL, are under distraction forces, and are high-risk for completion and displacement - they are a surgical urgency (urgent ortho, non-weight-bearing, usually percutaneous fixation).

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Tension-Side (Superolateral) Femoral Neck Stress Fracture - Surgical Urgency
textPreview: COMPRESSION-side fractures are INFEROMEDIAL, are under compressive load, and are usually managed conservatively (protected weight-bearing) when small - BUT a compression-side fracture line crossing >50% of the neck, or one that progresses or fails to settle on follow-up, also ...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Tension-Side (Superolateral) Femoral Neck Stress Fracture - Surgical Urgency
textPreview: Management/disposition pearl: a tension-side line, OR any fracture line involving more than ~50% of the neck width, mandates non-weight-bearing and urgent orthopedic referral for fixation. Do not let the patient walk out of the clinic or the scanner - mobilize them by wheelcha...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Tension-Side (Superolateral) Femoral Neck Stress Fracture - Surgical Urgency
textPreview: Pitfall: radiographs are frequently normal early - a normal X-ray does NOT exclude a femoral neck stress fracture. MRI is the standard for the occult high-risk hip.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Tension-Side (Superolateral) Femoral Neck Stress Fracture - Surgical Urgency
textPreview: Always look for and document the underlying driver (RED-S/female athlete triad, training-load spike); the fracture is a sentinel for a bone-health problem that will recur if unaddressed.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Avascular Necrosis of the Femoral Head (Double-Line Sign), Pre-Collapse
textPreview: The DOUBLE-LINE SIGN on T2 (inner bright granulation tissue + outer dark sclerosis) is pathognomonic for osteonecrosis and distinguishes AVN from transient bone-marrow edema, which lacks it.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Avascular Necrosis of the Femoral Head (Double-Line Sign), Pre-Collapse
textPreview: The single most important thing to determine is COLLAPSE: pre-collapse (no subchondral crescent, congruent surface) vs post-collapse (subchondral fracture/crescent, flattening). This is the management hinge.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Avascular Necrosis of the Femoral Head (Double-Line Sign), Pre-Collapse
textPreview: Management/disposition pearl: PRE-collapse AVN is the window for joint-preserving surgery (e.g., core decompression) - refer promptly. But efficacy is lesion-size dependent (best in small/medium lesions); large lesions involving the lateral weight-bearing pillar do poorly and ...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Avascular Necrosis of the Femoral Head (Double-Line Sign), Pre-Collapse
textPreview: Pitfall: AVN is bilateral in a large fraction of cases, especially steroid- and alcohol-associated - always image and explicitly comment on the contralateral hip, which may be asymptomatic but early and salvageable.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Gluteus Medius/Minimus Tendinopathy and Tear (Greater Trochanteric Pain Syndrome)
textPreview: Management/disposition pearl: a FULL-THICKNESS, RETRACTED abductor tear - especially with gluteal fatty atrophy (Goutallier/fatty infiltration) - shifts management from rehab/injection to surgical (orthopedic) referral for repair, because retracted, fatty-degenerated tendon is...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Proximal Hamstring Origin Tear/Avulsion at the Ischial Tuberosity
textPreview: Management/disposition pearl: a complete 3-tendon avulsion, or a 2-tendon avulsion with >2 cm retraction in an active patient, commonly triggers prompt surgical referral for possible primary repair. Partial or single-tendon injuries usually begin with rehabilitation, but persi...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Proximal Hamstring Origin Tear/Avulsion at the Ischial Tuberosity
textPreview: Pitfall: in skeletally immature athletes the injury is often an apophyseal AVULSION FRACTURE of the ischial tuberosity rather than a pure tendon tear - look for a displaced bony fragment and a different (often nonoperative-vs-operative by displacement) decision tree.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Proximal Hamstring Origin Tear/Avulsion at the Ischial Tuberosity
textPreview: Always comment on the SCIATIC NERVE: proximity to the retracted stump and hematoma matters for symptoms and for surgical planning; chronic scarring can tether the nerve.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Athletic Pubalgia / Core-Muscle Injury (Rectus Abdominis-Adductor Aponeurosis, Secondary Cleft Sign)
textPreview: Management/disposition pearl: athletic pubalgia is REHAB-first (progressive core and adductor strengthening, load management); image-guided injection and surgical core repair / adductor tenotomy are reserved for cases refractory to a dedicated rehab program.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Athletic Pubalgia / Core-Muscle Injury (Rectus Abdominis-Adductor Aponeurosis, Secondary Cleft Sign)
textPreview: Look for and grade the adductor longus origin: a full-thickness, RETRACTED adductor avulsion is a different, more surgical entity than the microtearing/tendinosis seen in athletic pubalgia.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Transient Bone-Marrow-Edema Syndrome vs Subchondral Insufficiency Fracture of the Femoral Head
textPreview: Both transient bone-marrow-edema syndrome and SIFK show diffuse femoral head edema - the DISCRIMINATOR is the subchondral fracture LINE. Transient BME has edema WITHOUT a subchondral fracture line or double-line and is self-limiting; SIFK has a subchondral fracture line and ri...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Transient Bone-Marrow-Edema Syndrome vs Subchondral Insufficiency Fracture of the Femoral Head
textPreview: Management/disposition pearl: transient BME syndrome is benign and self-limiting (protected weight-bearing, time, symptomatic care). SIFK is a structural fracture at risk of collapse - it needs protected weight-bearing, orthopedic referral, and short-interval follow-up to dete...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Transient Bone-Marrow-Edema Syndrome vs Subchondral Insufficiency Fracture of the Femoral Head
textPreview: Distinguish both from AVN: AVN has the serpiginous DOUBLE-LINE sign and a demarcated necrotic segment; transient BME and SIFK do not. Calling AVN incorrectly changes the entire prognosis conversation.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Transient Bone-Marrow-Edema Syndrome vs Subchondral Insufficiency Fracture of the Femoral Head
textPreview: Pitfall: the most dangerous error is over-reading diffuse edema as 'just transient BME' and missing the subtle low-signal subchondral fracture line of SIFK - scrutinize the anterosuperior subchondral region on every plane and on T1.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Transient Bone-Marrow-Edema Syndrome vs Subchondral Insufficiency Fracture of the Femoral Head
textPreview: SIFK is an INSUFFICIENCY fracture - look for and recommend workup of the underlying bone-quality problem (osteoporosis/osteopenia, vitamin D); the fracture is a marker of fragile bone.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Pincer-Type FAI with Acetabular Retroversion/Over-Coverage and Contrecoup Posteroinferior Chondral Injury
textPreview: Management/disposition pearl: symptomatic pincer FAI with chondrolabral injury warrants hip-preservation referral. The surgical nuance is that over-resection of a retroverted/over-covered rim risks iatrogenic instability/dysplasia - so report version and coverage carefully to ...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: A primary care sports medicine hip MRI course focused on management-changing interpretation: FAI and the labrum, cartilage, femoral-neck stress fracture and AVN, the abductor/hamstring/iliopsoas/adductor tendons, athletic pubalgia, and red flags. | Primary care sports medicine...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: Rim chondral delamination at the anterosuperior chondrolabral junction is the lesion that drives surgical decision-making in cam FAI; look for a fluid cleft undermining cartilage from subchondral bone, which conventional MRI routinely under-calls.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: MR arthrography with axial traction distracts the joint and opens the chondrolabral cleft, markedly improving detection and grading of cartilage delamination versus conventional MRA; an undermining fluid cleft indicates unstable delamination that reorders the surgical plan. Re...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: A superolateral (tension-side) femoral neck stress fracture is a surgical emergency: this side is under distraction, prone to displacement and AVN, and warrants urgent fixation referral — not the conservative course used for compression-side fractures.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: A compression-side (inferomedial) femoral neck stress fracture without a discrete fracture line can be managed with protected weight-bearing; but a fracture line crossing more than 50 percent of the neck width shifts the conversation toward fixation regardless of side.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: AVN's near-pathognomonic finding is the double-line sign on T2 — a hyperintense inner granulation line paralleled by a hypointense sclerotic outer line at the necrosis interface; its presence distinguishes AVN from nonspecific marrow edema.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: The hinge in AVN management is subchondral collapse: a subchondral fracture (crescent sign) or articular flattening marks ARCO stage III and ends the window for joint-preserving core decompression, pushing toward arthroplasty.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: Transient bone marrow edema syndrome shows diffuse femoral head and neck edema with NO subchondral fracture line and is self-limiting; a low-signal subchondral line instead points to subchondral insufficiency fracture, which carries collapse risk.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: Subchondral insufficiency fracture shows a serpiginous low-signal line parallel to the articular surface with surrounding edema, typically in older osteopenic patients; a line length >4 mm or any articular flattening predicts collapse.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: A full-thickness retracted gluteus medius/minimus tear with fatty muscle atrophy is the surgically relevant 'rotator cuff of the hip' lesion; measure the tendon gap and grade atrophy, because retraction plus high-grade fatty infiltration predicts irreparability and warrants re...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: Proximal hamstring avulsions hinge on tendon count and retraction: a complete 2-tendon (semitendinosus plus biceps) or 3-tendon avulsion with more than 2 cm retraction is a repair candidate, whereas partial or non-retracted tears are managed conservatively.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: A lesser trochanter avulsion in an adult without significant trauma is a pathologic fracture until proven otherwise — search the proximal femur and pelvis for an underlying metastasis or marrow-replacing lesion before calling it a simple iliopsoas avulsion.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: Greater trochanteric pain syndrome on MRI is a spectrum — bursal fluid, gluteal tendinosis, and footprint partial tears — and identifying an actual tendon tear rather than isolated bursitis is what redirects refractory cases toward targeted injection or surgery.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: Os acetabuli (an unfused or stress-related rim ossicle) can represent a chronic rim fracture in pincer FAI; distinguish it from a benign accessory ossification center, because a painful os acetabuli may be addressed at the time of rim trimming.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: On a hip effusion, a complex or T2-heterogeneous effusion with synovial thickening and rim enhancement after contrast should raise septic arthritis over a bland reactive effusion — aspirate rather than image again, because delay risks rapid cartilage destruction.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine

textPreview: When does MR arthrography change hip management versus non-contrast MRI? | Order MRA when a labral/chondral finding would push toward arthroscopy or alter the operative plan (FAI, suspected labral detachment/chondral delamination). For marrow (stress fracture, AVN, tumor), ten...

textPreview: On hip MRI, where do most pathologic labral tears and FAI rim chondral injuries occur, and why does location matter for management? | At the anterosuperior chondrolabral junction (weight-bearing zone). Anterosuperior disruption with adjacent cartilage delamination drives surgi...

textPreview: On hip MRI, which femoral-neck stress fracture location is a surgical emergency, and what is the immediate management? | A TENSION-side (superolateral) fracture line — high risk of completion/displacement and AVN. Make the patient non-weight-bearing and obtain urgent orthopedi...

title: How do you distinguish transient BME syndrome (BMES) from AVN and SIFK on MRI?
textPreview: How do you distinguish transient BME syndrome (BMES) from AVN and SIFK on MRI? | BMES = diffuse homogeneous marrow edema of the head/neck with NO subchondral fracture line, NO necrotic segment, and NO double-line sign; it is self-limiting. AVN shows a geographic lesion with a ...

title: A young female athlete has a sacral ala stress fracture on MRI. What additional work-up is essential?
textPreview: A young female athlete has a sacral ala stress fracture on MRI. What additional work-up is essential? | Screen for the female athlete triad / RED-S — relative energy deficiency, menstrual dysfunction, and low bone mineral density — and address bone health, since this underlies...

title: Why does rim chondral injury at the chondrolabral junction drive the FAI surgical decision?
textPreview: Why does rim chondral injury at the chondrolabral junction drive the FAI surgical decision? | It is the strongest imaging predictor of arthroscopic outcome. Reparable delamination/wave sign with an intact labrum favors osteochondroplasty + chondrolabral repair; diffuse full-th...

textPreview: On MRI, how does cam-shear chondral delamination differ from full-thickness loss, and why does it matter surgically? | Delamination = a deep cartilage-bone cleavage (thin fluid undercutting an intact, normal-thickness anterosuperior surface); full-thickness = a focal defect do...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What two numbers must a proximal hamstring avulsion report include for surgical planning?
textPreview: What two numbers must a proximal hamstring avulsion report include for surgical planning? | Tendon count (how many of the 3 tendons are torn) and retraction distance from the ischial tuberosity. A 2–3 tendon avulsion retracted >2 cm in an active patient favors surgical repair.

textPreview: On femoral-neck stress fracture, which side of the neck makes it a surgical emergency, and what do you do? | A fracture line on the SUPEROLATERAL (tension) side is unstable and a surgical emergency — make the patient non-weight-bearing and obtain urgent ortho referral. Compres...

textPreview: In femoral-head AVN, which MRI finding separates joint-preserving surgery from arthroplasty? | The subchondral fracture / crescent sign with loss of sphericity. Pre-collapse hips (intact contour, double-line sign only) can be offered core decompression; once the subchondral fr...

title: What is the highest-yield T1 check for distinguishing benign marrow from tumor replacement in the hip?
textPreview: What is the highest-yield T1 check for distinguishing benign marrow from tumor replacement in the hip? | Compare marrow to muscle/disc on T1. Normal red marrow is BRIGHTER than skeletal muscle; confluent marrow that is DARKER than muscle or disc is replaced/abnormal (tumor red...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Defaulting to MR arthrography for every labral question, or assuming it is always superior
textPreview: Ask whether the added detail changes the next step. Most 3T non-contrast hips answer the labral/chondral question; reserve direct arthrography (optionally with traction) for equivocal cases where confirming a tear or chondral delamination would shift management toward surgical...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Reading a herniation pit at the femoral neck as an aggressive lesion
textPreview: A herniation pit is a small, well-corticated subcortical cyst at the anterosuperior head-neck junction (T1-low/T2-high), classically benign and often a marker of cam-type FAI; note it but do not escalate to a tumor workup.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Stopping the read once a labral tear or trochanteric bursitis is found
textPreview: Labral tears are common and frequently incidental; the obvious finding is exactly when a tension-side femoral neck stress fracture, early AVN, marrow-replacing lesion, or sacral stress fracture gets missed. Finish all seven steps every time.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Calling all femoral-neck marrow edema a stress reaction and clearing the athlete to load
textPreview: Find and side the fracture line. A superolateral (tension-side) line is at high risk of completion and displacement: it is non-weight-bearing and an urgent orthopedic referral, not progressive loading. The side determines management and must be stated.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Reading marrow edema with a subchondral double line as simply transient osteoporosis
textPreview: A double-line sign (outer low-signal sclerotic rim + inner T2-high granulation line) and a serpiginous demarcated lesion indicate AVN, not BMES; BMES shows diffuse edema without a demarcating interface and is self-limited. Misclassification changes prognosis and follow-up.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Calling diffuse femoral-head/neck marrow edema pure BMES and stopping
textPreview: In an older or osteopenic patient, exclude a subchondral insufficiency fracture (low-signal subchondral line paralleling the cortex with surrounding edema); it carries a real collapse risk and is managed differently from self-limited BMES.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Reporting a labral tear but not searching the adjacent acetabular cartilage
textPreview: Anterosuperior labral tears are frequently accompanied by chondrolabral-junction chondral injury or delamination, which is often the more management-relevant finding for surgical planning and prognosis.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Attributing all lateral hip pain to 'trochanteric bursitis'
textPreview: GTPS is most often gluteus medius/minimus tendinopathy or tearing (the hip's rotator cuff), with peritrochanteric fluid usually secondary. Read the abductor footprint for tendon signal/thickness change and undersurface tears; a full-thickness retracted tear is the finding that...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Reporting a proximal hamstring tear without tendon count and retraction
textPreview: Management hinges on how many of the three tendons are involved and how far the stump has retracted. Acute multi-tendon avulsions with retraction are surgical-referral injuries; isolated tendinosis is rehab. Always state both, and note sciatic nerve proximity.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Dismissing hip/groin pain with negative radiographs as soft-tissue strain
textPreview: A negative radiograph does not exclude an occult femoral-neck fracture, tension-side stress fracture, AVN, or a marrow-replacing lesion. MRI (T1 plus fluid-sensitive) is the sensitive test; the tension-side fracture and occult elderly fracture change weight-bearing and referra...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Anchoring on a sports diagnosis and overlooking a marrow-replacing lesion or treating an effusion with synovitis as a degenerative flare
textPreview: Confluent low-T1 marrow that is darker than skeletal muscle or an intervertebral disc, or a soft-tissue mass/periosteal reaction, should trigger an oncologic workup. In the right clinical setting (fever, raised inflammatory markers), a tense effusion with synovitis and periart...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Treating a subtle SCFE as a benign physeal variant
textPreview: In an adolescent, even a subtle slipped capital femoral epiphysis is an orthopedic urgency: make non-weight-bearing and refer promptly to prevent further slip and AVN. Look for physeal widening/edema and posteromedial epiphyseal slip.

title: Femoral neck & head-neck junction
textPreview: ultrasound: seen: false | appearance: On US the anterior femoral neck and head-neck junction read as a smooth hyperechoic cortical curve — a useful landmark that forms the floor of the anterior recess and the target for joint aspiration/injection. But marrow, a femoral-neck st...

title: Iliopsoas tendon & bursa
textPreview: ultrasound: seen: true | appearance: The iliopsoas tendon is well seen as a rounded hyperechoic structure anterior to the femoral head/acetabular (iliopectineal) eminence and just lateral to the femoral vessels, with the muscle belly wrapping behind it; the iliopsoas bursa is ...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Sciatic nerve
textPreview: ultrasound: seen: true | appearance: US shows the sciatic nerve as a flat hyperechoic honeycomb (fascicular) band in the subgluteal space, between the ischial tuberosity and the greater trochanter, deep to gluteus maximus as it passes beneath piriformis. It is the modality for...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Femoral head
textPreview: Watch for a serpiginous subchondral double-line (AVN) or a focal subchondral low-signal fracture band with surrounding edema (SIFH) — both can appear before the head loses its sphericity, and the water image is where the edema becomes obvious.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Acetabulum & sourcil
textPreview: Watch for sourcil subchondral edema or a thin bright line beneath it on the water image — early stress response or insufficiency fracture in the weight-bearing roof, not just degenerative sclerosis.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Femoral neck & head-neck junction
textPreview: Watch the SUPEROLATERAL (tension) cortex of the neck — a stress fracture there is the surgical-urgency miss prone to displacement; the inferomedial (compression) side is the lower-risk pattern.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Sacrum & SI joint
textPreview: Watch for unilateral periarticular SI marrow edema (inflammatory sacroiliitis) or an H-/butterfly-shaped (Honda sign) sacral edema pattern — the insufficiency fracture that masquerades as 'hip' pain in runners and older patients.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Sciatic nerve
textPreview: Watch for nerve enlargement or bright edema-like signal as it passes the piriformis or hamstring origin — the clue to piriformis (deep gluteal) syndrome or proximal-hamstring-avulsion entrapment.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Femoral head
textPreview: variant: Do not treat marrow edema as a diagnosis. AVN is a geographic/serpiginous subchondral lesion often with a T2 double-line; SIF is a low-signal subchondral fracture band paralleling the articular surface; transient marrow edema lacks a discrete fracture/necrosis line.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Acetabulum & sourcil
textPreview: variant: A corticated os acetabuli with smooth margins at the anterosuperior rim is a chronic rim variant/fragment, not an acute fracture (sharp margins + marrow edema). | measure: Read coverage by the lateral center-edge angle on radiographs: <20° = dysplasia/undercoverage, 2...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Sacrum & SI joint
textPreview: variant: Bilateral symmetric tiny SI-margin signal or degenerative change is not automatically inflammatory sacroiliitis. Confirm true periarticular marrow edema/erosions and keep sacral-ala insufficiency fracture in the differential for buttock or groin pain.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Sciatic nerve
textPreview: variant: Seeing the sciatic nerve is normal. Deep-gluteal entrapment needs a clinical posterior-pain pattern plus nerve enlargement, T2 hyperintensity, fat-plane loss, or a compressive lesion.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Joint capsule & iliofemoral ligament
textPreview: variant: A thin, concave anterior capsular margin is normal. Convex distension supports effusion/synovitis; capsular thickening or laxity should be interpreted with symptoms and surgical/instability history.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Femoral head
textPreview: On this coronal T2 FS Dixon the femoral head is the rounded epiphysis seated in the acetabulum; on the water (fat-suppressed) image its normal fatty marrow is uniformly dark because the marrow fat signal has been nulled. Confirm the head is spherical and that the marrow stays ...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Femoral neck & head-neck junction
textPreview: The femoral neck connects head to trochanters; on this coronal slice assess the marrow signal and both cortices and follow the head-neck contour up to the junction. The tension (superolateral) side of the neck is the high-stakes location — it is where the dangerous stress frac...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Do-not-overcall checkpoint
textPreview: Before leaving the coronal stack, pause on the traps: cam or pincer morphology is not symptomatic FAI without concordant pain plus labral/cartilage injury; GTPS is usually abductor tendon disease, not bursal fluid alone; pubic marrow or aponeurotic signal can be seen in asympt...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Get oriented
textPreview: Axial T2 FS, dedicated single (left) hip. Orient yourself with the soft-tissue landmarks rather than by eye: the round dark iliopsoas tendon sits ANTERIOR to the femoral head, and the fascicular sciatic nerve lies POSTERIOR between the ischium and the greater trochanter. This ...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Femoral head
textPreview: On axial T2 FS the femoral head is the round, spherical ball seated in the acetabular socket — check its sphericity and confirm uniformly dark (fat-suppressed) marrow with no bright edema. It is the screening target for AVN (subchondral edema, later a serpiginous double-line) ...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Sciatic nerve
textPreview: On axial T2 FS the sciatic nerve is the rounded, fascicular soft-tissue structure posterior to the hip, lying just posterior to the ischium between the ischial tuberosity and greater trochanter, deep to gluteus maximus. Normal: smooth fascicular nerve, not enlarged or hyperint...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Do-not-overcall checkpoint
textPreview: On axial, do not call every labral-base cleft a tear: posteroinferior sublabral sulci/perilabral sulci and labral-shape variation are common pitfalls. Iliopsoas bursal fluid may communicate with the joint, so it can be secondary. Sciatic-neuralgia imaging needs nerve enlargeme...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Femoral head
textPreview: On this sagittal PD FS the femoral head is the large round epiphysis capped by smooth low-signal articular cartilage and seated in the acetabulum. Confirm it is perfectly spherical with normal marrow (fatty signal that suppresses uniformly on this fat-sat sequence) and an inta...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Femoral neck & head-neck junction
textPreview: The sagittal plane profiles the femoral neck and shows the anterior head-neck junction contour. Trace a smooth concave anterior waist from head to neck — loss of that concavity (a convex 'bump') suggests cam morphology, though the alpha angle itself is measured on oblique-axia...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Tension-side femoral-neck stress fracture
textPreview: A line on the SUPEROLATERAL (tension) cortex is the surgical-urgency pattern — prone to displacement and AVN. Protected/non-weight-bearing + prompt ortho.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: AVN pre-collapse (double-line sign)
textPreview: A radiograph-negative double-line is ARCO I — the joint-preservation window. Refer promptly and image the contralateral hip (often bilateral).

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Subchondral insufficiency fracture (SIFK)
textPreview: A subchondral low-signal fracture line (± flattening) carries collapse risk — it is NOT benign transient marrow edema. Offload and refer.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Marrow-replacing lesion
textPreview: Low-T1 marrow that isn't a stress/edema pattern — tumor or infiltration until proven otherwise.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Proximal hamstring avulsion
textPreview: ≥2 tendons torn AND ≥2 cm retraction meets the surgical-referral window (the sciatic nerve sits right there) — flag it before retraction worsens.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Planes — what each one answers
textPreview: label: Large-FOV coronal (both hips + pelvis) | detail: The screen. T2 FS Dixon across both hips, sacrum, and symphysis catches marrow lesions, occult/stress fractures, AVN, SI joints, and athletic pubalgia — and lets you compare sides. Use this first when pain is poorly local...

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Sequences — what they're for
textPreview: label: Fluid-sensitive fat-sat (T2 FS / STIR) | detail: Edema, marrow injury, effusion, stress-fracture lines, and AVN reactive edema. A finding is more convincing when fluid-bright signal points to it — read these on every step. | label: T1-weighted | detail: Anatomy, marrow ...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Degenerative Knee — Tricompartmental Osteoarthritis
textPreview: Degenerative meniscal tears in the setting of OA rarely benefit from arthroscopic surgery — multiple RCTs show no benefit over physical therapy.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Degenerative Knee — Tricompartmental Osteoarthritis
textPreview: When reporting OA, describe compartment-by-compartment severity to help surgical planning (unicompartmental vs total knee arthroplasty).

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: ACL Tear + Pivot-Shift Pattern
textPreview: A Segond fracture (lateral capsular avulsion) is highly specific for associated ACL injury and should prompt direct ACL assessment.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: ACL Tear + Pivot-Shift Pattern
textPreview: Lipohemarthrosis (fat-fluid level on axial images) indicates an intra-articular fracture.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patellar Dislocation + MPFL Tear
textPreview: The patellar dislocation contusion pattern is medial patellar facet + anterolateral LFC — these are "kissing contusions."

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patellar Dislocation + MPFL Tear
textPreview: Always look for osteochondral fragments and loose bodies after patellar dislocation.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: OCD Stability Assessment
textPreview: Adult (De Smet) MRI instability criteria: (1) high-T2 rim at the fragment–parent-bone interface, (2) fluid (matching fluid signal) undercutting the fragment, (3) cyst-like lesions beneath the fragment, (4) a focal articular-cartilage breach. JUVENILE caveat: in skeletally imma...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: OCD Stability Assessment
textPreview: Juvenile OCD (open physes) has better healing potential than adult OCD — but unstable fragments still often need surgery.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: OCD Stability Assessment
textPreview: Always measure the lesion size and report stability features — this directly drives surgical decision-making.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: SIFK / SONK
textPreview: SIFK (formerly SONK) is NOT true osteonecrosis — it is a subchondral insufficiency fracture that may progress to secondary osteonecrosis.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: SIFK / SONK
textPreview: The key MRI finding is a low-signal subchondral fracture line on T1, surrounded by extensive marrow edema.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: SIFK / SONK
textPreview: Differential diagnosis: OCD (younger; classically the lateral aspect of the medial femoral condyle), AVN (bilateral, multifocal), stress fracture (linear orientation).

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Multiligament Knee Injury
textPreview: A knee dislocation is usually a bicruciate injury (ACL + PCL) and still counts when the knee has spontaneously reduced; the uncommon Schenck KD-I pattern is a documented tibiofemoral dislocation with only one cruciate torn.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Multiligament Knee Injury
textPreview: The Schenck (KD) classification: KD-I (single cruciate torn — ACL or PCL — with the other cruciate intact; ± collateral), KD-II (both cruciates, collaterals intact), KD-III (both cruciates + one collateral; IIIM = medial, IIIL = lateral), KD-IV (both cruciates + both collatera...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Multiligament Knee Injury
textPreview: Popliteal artery injury can be occult in knee dislocation — urgent vascular assessment is required; CTA/MRA is appropriate when pulses, ABI, or clinical findings are abnormal or equivocal.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patellar Tendon Rupture
textPreview: Patellar tendon rupture is more common in patients <40; quadriceps tendon rupture is more common >40.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patellar Tendon Rupture
textPreview: Patella alta (high-riding patella) is a hallmark of patellar tendon rupture — measure the Insall-Salvati ratio (normal ~1.0, alta >1.2).

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
textPreview: The posterior horn of the medial meniscus is normally the largest segment and noticeably larger than the anterior horn (roughly twice the size, as a rule of thumb). If it appears small or absent, consider a displaced tear or root avulsion.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
textPreview: After patellar dislocation, search for an osteochondral fracture (medial patellar facet / lateral femoral condyle), seen in up to ~50% of cases, AND for a displaced loose body in the suprapatellar pouch or notch — less frequent (~15–30%) but management-changing.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
textPreview: SIFK (subchondral insufficiency fracture of the knee) is associated with posterior medial meniscal root tears and extrusion, which can increase compartment loading — always check the root.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
textPreview: The Segond fracture (small lateral tibial avulsion) is highly specific for associated ACL injury. Look for it on coronal images, then assess the ACL directly.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
textPreview: Bone marrow edema on T1 appears dark, but a discrete low-signal LINE within the edema = fracture (not just contusion). This changes weight-bearing protocol.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
textPreview: OCD stability: fluid signal at the fragment-parent bone interface, cystic change, or displacement = unstable. Surrounding edema alone does NOT mean unstable.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
textPreview: Large lateral parameniscal cysts near the fibular head can compress the common peroneal nerve, presenting with foot drop.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
textPreview: In multi-ligament knee injuries (ACL + PCL), always evaluate the posterolateral corner (LCL, popliteus, popliteofibular ligament). PLC injury changes surgical timing.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
textPreview: Bipartite patella: smooth, corticated margins, classically superolateral. Fracture: irregular, non-corticated margins with edema and effusion.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
textPreview: Lipohemarthrosis (fat-fluid level on axial images) indicates an intra-articular fracture, even if you can’t see the fracture line.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
