courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is Hoffa's fat pad impingement?
textPreview: What is Hoffa's fat pad impingement? | Hoffa's fat pad (infrapatellar fat pad) lies behind the patellar tendon, below the patella, and in front of the femoral trochlea and tibia. It can become inflamed and edematous from repetitive anterior-compartment impingement — classicall...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: When should I evaluate the popliteal artery?
textPreview: When should I evaluate the popliteal artery? | Evaluate the popliteal artery on MRI when you see multiligament injuries (especially bicruciate), tibial plateau fractures with posterior cortex involvement, or knee dislocation. The popliteal artery is tethered as it passes behin...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: On T1-weighted images, what does normal marrow look like and what causes it to appear dark?
textPreview: On T1-weighted images, what does normal marrow look like and what causes it to appear dark? | Normal marrow is fatty and therefore bright on T1. Any process that replaces marrow fat (fracture, tumor, infection, edema) appears dark on T1.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the Segond fracture and what is its clinical significance?
textPreview: What is the Segond fracture and what is its clinical significance? | A small avulsion fracture of the lateral tibial rim. It is highly specific for associated ACL injury and should prompt direct ACL evaluation.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the significance of a tibial eminence fracture in an adolescent?
textPreview: What is the significance of a tibial eminence fracture in an adolescent? | It represents an avulsion equivalent of an ACL tear.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How do you differentiate a bone contusion from a fracture on MRI?
textPreview: How do you differentiate a bone contusion from a fracture on MRI? | Both show bone marrow edema on fluid-sensitive sequences. A fracture has a discrete low-signal line on T1 within the edema; a contusion does not. This distinction changes weight-bearing status.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the classic bone bruise pattern of lateral patellar dislocation?
textPreview: What is the classic bone bruise pattern of lateral patellar dislocation? | Edema on the medial patellar facet and the anterolateral aspect of the lateral femoral condyle. This pattern results from the patella impacting the lateral condyle during transient dislocation.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is SIFK (SONK) and how does it differ from OCD?
textPreview: What is SIFK (SONK) and how does it differ from OCD? | SIFK (subchondral insufficiency fracture of the knee) occurs in older patients (typically women >55) with acute-onset pain and shows subchondral low-signal fracture line with surrounding edema, often in the medial femoral ...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What single imaging check separates juvenile from adult OCD, and why does it matter?
textPreview: What single imaging check separates juvenile from adult OCD, and why does it matter? | The distal femoral physis on coronal images: OPEN physes = juvenile OCD (greater healing potential, more often managed conservatively); CLOSED physes = adult OCD (poorer healing, more often ...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the root tear triad?
textPreview: What is the root tear triad? | Posterior medial root tear + meniscal extrusion (>3 mm) + subchondral insufficiency fracture of the medial femoral condyle or tibial plateau. The root tear is the underlying biomechanical cause.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What complication can a large lateral parameniscal cyst near the fibular head cause?
textPreview: What complication can a large lateral parameniscal cyst near the fibular head cause? | Compression of the common peroneal nerve, presenting with foot drop.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What are the primary MRI signs of an ACL tear?
textPreview: What are the primary MRI signs of an ACL tear? | Discontinuity of ligament fibers, abnormal morphology (wavy, lax, or horizontally oriented), and abnormal signal intensity (edema/hemorrhage on T2). A complete tear shows amorphous edematous tissue replacing the ligament.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Name three secondary signs of an ACL tear.
textPreview: Name three secondary signs of an ACL tear. | Pivot-shift bone contusions (posterolateral tibial plateau + lateral femoral condyle sulcus), anterior tibial translation >5 mm, and PCL buckling. Others include deepened lateral femoral sulcus >1.5 mm, Segond fracture, and uncovere...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the Segond fracture and what is its association with ACL tears?
textPreview: What is the Segond fracture and what is its association with ACL tears? | A small avulsion of the lateral tibial rim representing anterolateral-ligament complex avulsion. It is highly specific for associated ACL injury, reported with ACL injury in approximately 75-100% of case...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What are the three grades of MCL injury and their MRI findings?
textPreview: What are the three grades of MCL injury and their MRI findings? | Grade I (sprain): periligamentous edema with intact fibers. Grade II (partial tear): partial discontinuity with surrounding edema. Grade III (complete tear): complete fiber disruption with fluid filling the gap.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Why is it important to specify whether an MCL injury involves the superficial or deep layer?
textPreview: Why is it important to specify whether an MCL injury involves the superficial or deep layer? | The deep MCL (meniscotibial ligament) attaches directly to the medial meniscus. Deep MCL tears can cause meniscocapsular separation, which may require surgical repair -- a different ...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the arcuate fracture and what does it indicate?
textPreview: What is the arcuate fracture and what does it indicate? | Avulsion of the fibular styloid. It is a strong marker of posterolateral-corner injury and should trigger direct evaluation of the LCL, popliteus tendon, and popliteofibular ligament.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What bone bruise pattern is classic for transient lateral patellar dislocation?
textPreview: What bone bruise pattern is classic for transient lateral patellar dislocation? | Edema on the medial patellar facet and the anterolateral aspect of the lateral femoral condyle.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What three anatomic risk factors for recurrent patellar dislocation should be assessed?
textPreview: What three anatomic risk factors for recurrent patellar dislocation should be assessed? | Patella alta (Insall-Salvati ratio >1.2), trochlear dysplasia (Dejour classification), and increased TT-TG distance (>20 mm is pathologic).

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What combination of ligament tears constitutes a knee dislocation until proven otherwise?
textPreview: What combination of ligament tears constitutes a knee dislocation until proven otherwise? | Combined ACL + PCL disruption, even if the knee has spontaneously reduced. This carries a limb-threatening risk of occult popliteal artery injury, requiring urgent vascular assessment.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What vascular injury must be excluded in a multiligament knee injury, and how should it be assessed?
textPreview: What vascular injury must be excluded in a multiligament knee injury, and how should it be assessed? | Popliteal artery injury must be excluded urgently. MRI signs include loss of the normal flow void, perivascular hematoma, and intimal irregularity; CTA/MRA is used when pulse...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What finding on MRI distinguishes a complete quadriceps tendon tear from a partial tear?
textPreview: What finding on MRI distinguishes a complete quadriceps tendon tear from a partial tear? | A complete tear shows full-thickness discontinuity with proximal retraction of the tendon stump, patella baja (low-riding patella), and often a large hemorrhagic joint effusion.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How does patellar tendinopathy (jumper's knee) differ from a partial patellar tendon tear on MRI?
textPreview: How does patellar tendinopathy (jumper's knee) differ from a partial patellar tendon tear on MRI? | Tendinopathy shows thickening and intermediate signal (not fluid-bright on T2) with intact continuity. A partial tear shows a discrete fluid-signal defect within the tendon. Thi...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How do you differentiate bipartite patella from a patellar fracture?
textPreview: How do you differentiate bipartite patella from a patellar fracture? | Bipartite patella has smooth, well-corticated margins, is located at the superolateral pole (~75% of cases), and is bilateral in ~50%. Fractures have sharp, irregular margins, most commonly occur at the mid...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What does a lipohemarthrosis indicate and how does it appear on MRI?
textPreview: What does a lipohemarthrosis indicate and how does it appear on MRI? | Lipohemarthrosis is pathognomonic for an intra-articular fracture. It appears as a fat-fluid level in the suprapatellar bursa: the superior fat layer is bright on T1, and the inferior layer is serous or hem...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: When you identify a lipohemarthrosis but no obvious fracture, what should you do?
textPreview: When you identify a lipohemarthrosis but no obvious fracture, what should you do? | Perform a dedicated second-look fracture search of the tibial plateaus, patella, and femoral condyles. Subtle lateral tibial plateau depression fractures are the most commonly missed finding.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the hallmark MRI finding of PVNS (tenosynovial giant cell tumor)?
textPreview: What is the hallmark MRI finding of PVNS (tenosynovial giant cell tumor)? | Low signal intensity on all pulse sequences due to hemosiderin deposition, with "blooming" artifact on gradient echo sequences. Diffuse PVNS also shows bone erosions on both sides of the joint with rel...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What are the two tendinous components of the proximal hamstring origin at the ischial tuberosity, and what must the report specify in an avulsion?
textPreview: What are the two tendinous components of the proximal hamstring origin at the ischial tuberosity, and what must the report specify in an avulsion? | (1) The conjoint tendon (biceps femoris long head + semitendinosus fused into one tendon) and (2) the separate semimembranosus t...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the signature MRI finding of medial gastrocnemius 'tennis leg,' and which two diagnoses must always be on the differential?
textPreview: What is the signature MRI finding of medial gastrocnemius 'tennis leg,' and which two diagnoses must always be on the differential? | Fluid tracking between the medial gastrocnemius and the soleus aponeurosis at the distal myotendinous junction. Always exclude DVT (check the p...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: When you see a subchondral insufficiency fracture of the medial femoral condyle, what must you evaluate?
textPreview: When you see a subchondral insufficiency fracture of the medial femoral condyle, what must you evaluate? | The posterior medial meniscal root. The root tear is the underlying biomechanical cause. If you only report the fracture without identifying the root tear, the underlying...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the most reliable MRI sign of OCD fragment instability, and why is T1 signal unreliable for this assessment?
textPreview: What is the most reliable MRI sign of OCD fragment instability, and why is T1 signal unreliable for this assessment? | Fluid-signal intensity (matching joint fluid) on T2 at the fragment-bone interface indicates undermining and instability. A low-signal rim on T1 is nonspecifi...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What fracture is a strong marker of posterolateral-corner injury?
textPreview: What fracture is a strong marker of posterolateral-corner injury? | Arcuate fracture -- avulsion of the fibular styloid. It should trigger direct evaluation of every PLC component rather than being dismissed as incidental.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: After diagnosing lateral patellar dislocation with MPFL tear, what is the complete evaluation checklist?
textPreview: After diagnosing lateral patellar dislocation with MPFL tear, what is the complete evaluation checklist? | MPFL tear location, osteochondral injury and loose bodies (check suprapatellar pouch, lateral gutter, posterior recesses), trochlear dysplasia, patella alta (Insall-Salva...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What finding is pathognomonic for an intra-articular fracture?
textPreview: What finding is pathognomonic for an intra-articular fracture? | Lipohemarthrosis (fat-fluid level in the suprapatellar bursa). When identified, perform a systematic search for fractures of the tibial plateau, patella, and femoral condyles.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is a Segond fracture, and how does a reverse Segond fracture differ in its ligamentous association?
textPreview: What is a Segond fracture, and how does a reverse Segond fracture differ in its ligamentous association? | Segond fracture: lateral tibial rim avulsion, highly specific for associated ACL injury. Reverse Segond fracture: medial tibial rim avulsion, associated with PCL injury r...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Why does combined ACL + PCL disruption require emergent vascular assessment?
textPreview: Why does combined ACL + PCL disruption require emergent vascular assessment? | Combined cruciate disruption equals a knee dislocation pattern until proven otherwise. Popliteal artery injury can be occult after spontaneous reduction, so urgent vascular assessment is required; C...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What three key questions should your knee MRI report answer for the surgeon?
textPreview: What three key questions should your knee MRI report answer for the surgeon? | (1) What is the diagnosis? (2) What associated injuries are present? (3) Does anything change my planned approach? Anticipate the surgeon's decision points and address them proactively.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: For meniscal tears, what factors should you address to help the surgeon determine repairability?
textPreview: For meniscal tears, what factors should you address to help the surgeon determine repairability? | Location (peripheral red zone = repairable vs. central white zone = resection), morphology (vertical longitudinal = repairable vs. complex = usually resection), displacement stat...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Name four findings on knee MRI that require urgent or direct communication with the referring clinician.
textPreview: Name four findings on knee MRI that require urgent or direct communication with the referring clinician. | Knee dislocation/multiligament injury (needs urgent vascular assessment), locked bucket-handle tear, displaced osteochondral fragments in a weight-bearing compartment, an...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Starting your report before completing the full search pattern
textPreview: Complete all 7 steps before dictating. The clinical question may focus on the ACL, but the ramp lesion or root tear you find at step 4 changes surgical planning.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Skipping Step 6 (synovium/bursae) because 'nothing is usually there'
textPreview: Baker cyst rupture, PVNS, plicae, and periarticular bursitis are often incidental but clinically important findings that change management.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Missing a subtle fracture line within bone marrow edema
textPreview: Always check T1 for a discrete low-signal line within the edema. Edema alone is a contusion; a line within edema is a fracture — which changes weight-bearing status.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Calling all subchondral femoral condyle lesions 'OCD'
textPreview: SIFK in a 60-year-old woman is NOT OCD. Check age, location, onset, and associated meniscal root tear. The differential (SIFK vs OCD vs AVN) has completely different management.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Calling an OCD 'unstable' based on surrounding edema alone
textPreview: Edema is common in both stable and unstable lesions. Instability requires fluid signal between the fragment and parent bone, cystic change at the interface, or a displaced fragment.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Grading an MCL injury without specifying superficial vs deep layer involvement
textPreview: The deep MCL (meniscotibial ligament) attaches to the meniscus. Its injury can cause meniscal displacement and has different surgical implications than superficial MCL injury.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Missing a posterolateral corner injury because you focused on the ACL
textPreview: Satisfaction of search! In ACL + PCL injuries, always check: fibular collateral ligament, popliteus tendon, popliteofibular ligament, and the arcuate complex. PLC injuries change surgical timing.

title: Ramp lesions in ACL injuries
textPreview: ### Ramp Lesions **Ramp lesions** (meniscocapsular tears of the posterior medial meniscus) are missed on preoperative MRI in **up to 50% of cases**, making them one of the most underdiagnosed findings in knee imaging. They are present in approximately **16--24% of ACL-injured ...

title: Bucket-handle tears (displaced fragment)
textPreview: ### Bucket-Handle Tears **Bucket-handle tears** represent displaced vertical longitudinal meniscal tears where the central fragment flips into the **intercondylar notch**. They cause **mechanical locking** and require **urgent surgical intervention**. Missing a bucket-handle t...

textPreview: ### Unstable Osteochondral Defects (OCD) **Unstable osteochondral defects** are critical to identify because they determine whether surgical intervention is needed. The distinction between **stable** and **unstable** fundamentally changes management: - **Stable** lesions may b...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Lipohemarthrosis (occult fracture)
textPreview: ### Lipohemarthrosis **Lipohemarthrosis** (a fat–fluid level) on knee MRI indicates an **intra-articular fracture until proven otherwise** and requires an immediate systematic search for the fracture source. It is best seen on a **non–fat-suppressed** sequence — fat saturation...

textPreview: ### Segond Fracture The **Segond fracture** is a small avulsion fracture of the **lateral tibial rim**, representing avulsion of the **anterolateral ligament (ALL)** or lateral capsular structures. It is a **highly specific marker of associated ACL injury**, reported with an A...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Anterior cruciate ligament
textPreview: ultrasound: seen: false | appearance: The cruciates are deep and intra-articular, so standard knee ultrasound cannot reliably image the ACL — MRI (or arthroscopy) is the modality. US may show only indirect clues such as an effusion or a Segond cortical avulsion fragment.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Lateral collateral ligament (LCL)
textPreview: ultrasound: seen: true | appearance: A discrete cord-like ligament from the lateral femoral condyle to the fibular head. Trace the rest of the posterolateral corner on adjacent images — the biceps femoris, popliteus tendon, and popliteofibular ligament. | tip: Coronal long-axi...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Tibial plateau
textPreview: ultrasound: seen: false | appearance: Subchondral and marrow injury of the plateau are MRI findings; US sees only the cortical surface and adjacent soft tissue, not the marrow or a non-displaced fracture line.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: MPFL / medial retinaculum
textPreview: ultrasound: seen: true | appearance: The MPFL runs from the superomedial patella toward the adductor tubercle; after a lateral patellar dislocation US can show a tear at the patellar attachment as hypoechoic disruption. | tip: Transverse at the superomedial patella, following ...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Tibial plateau
textPreview: Scan the marrow for a discrete dark fracture line; pivot-shift contusions sit at the posterolateral plateau.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patellar tendon
textPreview: Normally a few mm thick and uniformly dark; focal thickening = tendinopathy (usually proximal), a fluid-filled gap = rupture.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Femoral trochlea
textPreview: A shallow or flat trochlea (dysplasia) is the key predisposing factor for lateral patellar dislocation.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: MPFL / medial retinaculum
textPreview: After a dislocation the MPFL tears at the medial patella or the femoral (adductor tubercle) end — look at both.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Bone marrow — T1's job
textPreview: A dark band within bright marrow = fracture; diffuse marrow darkening = infiltration. T1 is your marrow sequence.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Patella
textPreview: variant: A subchondral lucency in the SUPEROLATERAL quadrant with intact overlying cartilage is the benign dorsal defect of the patella — don't call it an osteochondral lesion or infection.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Femoral trochlea
textPreview: measure: Trochlear dysplasia and elevated TT-TG are separate instability risk markers. TT-TG >20 mm is the classic abnormal cutoff, but MRI values vary with landmarks, slice choice, and knee position — do not use one number as a standalone surgical rule.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Femoral condyle
textPreview: variant: An OCD interface line only as bright as cartilage (not fluid), especially in a skeletally immature knee, suggests granulation tissue and a STABLE fragment; a fluid-bright (T2) rim fully undercutting it means unstable.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Tibial plateau
textPreview: The flat top of the tibia below the joint line. Marrow signal should be uniform — a discrete dark line within it would signal a fracture. | Tibial plateau

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Popliteal vessels
textPreview: The popliteal artery and vein run vertically in the popliteal fossa behind the joint — a useful posterior landmark. Don't mistake the flow-related signal for pathology. | Popliteal vessels

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Lateral collateral ligament (LCL)
textPreview: The cord-like LCL runs down the lateral edge from the lateral femoral epicondyle to the fibular head — the lateral counterpart of the MCL. The popliteus and posterolateral corner are confirmed on more posterior slices. | Lateral collateral ligament (LCL)

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: MPFL / medial retinaculum
textPreview: The medial patellofemoral ligament runs from the medial patella toward the adductor tubercle — the main restraint against lateral dislocation, and what tears after a patellar dislocation. | MPFL / medial retinaculum

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Popliteal vessels
textPreview: The popliteal artery and vein in the popliteal fossa posteriorly — a reliable posterior landmark. | Popliteal vessels

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Tibial plateau
textPreview: Uniform bright marrow below the joint. A discrete DARK line within the bright marrow is the classic T1 sign of a fracture. | Tibial plateau

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Bone marrow — T1's job
textPreview: Normal marrow is uniformly bright (fatty) on T1. The key abnormal finding is FOCAL DARK signal — a fracture line, infiltration, or a lesion. (Joint fluid is dark on T1, so it is the PD-FS — not T1 — that you use for cartilage and effusion.) | Bone marrow

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Popliteal vessels
textPreview: The popliteal artery and vein in the popliteal fossa posteriorly. | Popliteal vessels

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Bicruciate injury / knee dislocation
textPreview: Even if spontaneously reduced — clear the popliteal artery (flow voids) and common peroneal nerve. A missed vascular injury threatens the limb. Urgent.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Locked knee + displaced bucket-handle tear
textPreview: Double-PCL sign or fewer than two meniscal bow-ties = a fragment in the notch blocking extension — mechanical, needs surgery, not a brace.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Extensor mechanism rupture (quadriceps / patellar tendon)
textPreview: Full-thickness gap + can't extend = surgical repair. Don't undercall a high-grade partial tear.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Marrow-replacing lesion
textPreview: Low-T1 marrow that isn't a contusion — tumor or infiltration until proven otherwise. Don't write it off as edema.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Segond / reverse Segond fracture
textPreview: A lateral tibial rim avulsion points to an ACL tear (~75–100%); a posteromedial avulsion points to the PCL. The fracture flags the ligament — go look.

title: Extensor Mechanism

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Anterior Instability with Bankart and Hill-Sachs Lesions
textPreview: Do not stop at Bankart; inspect for bony Bankart, Hill-Sachs, HAGL, cuff tear, and loose body.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Anterior Instability with Bankart and Hill-Sachs Lesions
textPreview: Primary care sports medicine should recognize bone loss patterns that make surgical consultation more likely.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Superior Labrum/Biceps Anchor Injury in an Overhead Athlete
textPreview: Confidence calibration: labral interpretation (especially SLAP and posterosuperior variants) has only moderate inter-observer agreement even among experts. A borderline MRI finding should NOT drive surgery unless imaging, exam, sport, and symptoms all align.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Upper Subscapularis Tear with Medial Biceps Subluxation (the “hidden lesion”)
textPreview: Naming “upper subscapularis tear + medial biceps subluxation” changes the surgical conversation far more than “rotator cuff tendinopathy.”

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Massive Chronic Cuff Tear with Cuff Tear Arthropathy
textPreview: Primary care sports medicine should recognize this as a referral pattern but with different expectations than acute repairable tear.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: Grade articular-surface partial tears by depth: a defect greater than 50% of the ~12 mm footprint (a high-grade PASTA lesion, roughly >6 mm) generally crosses the surgical threshold for repair rather than debridement.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: The anteroinferior labroligamentous complex (3-6 o'clock) is the instability hot spot: a Bankart tear here in a young patient after anterior dislocation predicts recurrence. Trace the IGHL anterior band to its labral attachment.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: A HAGL lesion (humeral avulsion of the glenohumeral ligament) produces a 'J-sign' axillary pouch on coronal images and is a commonly missed cause of instability — it is repaired on the humeral side, not at the labrum, so it must be reported.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: Quantify glenoid bone loss before instability surgery: roughly >20-25% loss favors a bony augmentation (Latarjet) over isolated soft-tissue Bankart repair. CT with 3D 'en-face' glenoid reconstruction is the reference standard for measurement.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: A Hill-Sachs lesion is a posterolateral humeral head impaction fracture, typically at or above the level of the coracoid; the normal bare area lies more inferiorly. Measure its width and depth and pair it with the bony Bankart to determine on-track versus off-track status.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: An empty bicipital groove on axial images means the long head of biceps has dislocated (usually medially, with a subscapularis tear) or fully ruptured and retracted. Follow the tendon distally toward the myotendinous junction to distinguish dislocation from rupture.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: Quadrilateral space syndrome shows isolated fatty atrophy or denervation edema of the teres minor (± deltoid) from axillary nerve compression. Isolated teres minor changes should prompt a search for a paralabral cyst, fibrous band, or mass in the quadrilateral space.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: Parsonage-Turner (neuralgic amyotrophy) presents as diffuse T2 denervation edema across muscles in a non-compressive, often multi-nerve distribution (commonly supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and deltoid) with no compressive mass — pain preceding weakness is the clinical tip-off.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: A spinoglenoid-notch paralabral cyst compresses the suprascapular nerve distally, denervating the infraspinatus alone; a suprascapular-notch cyst affects both supraspinatus and infraspinatus. Always trace a posterior paralabral cyst to its parent posterosuperior labral tear.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: The Buford complex is a congenital variant: absent anterosuperior labrum with a thick, cord-like middle glenohumeral ligament. Recognize it and do NOT surgically reattach the 'detached' labrum, which would restrict external rotation.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: A reverse Hill-Sachs (anteromedial humeral head impaction) plus a reverse Bankart (posteroinferior labral tear) signals prior posterior dislocation — easily overlooked because the humeral head can look 'reduced.' Suspect it after seizures, electrocution, or a posteriorly direc...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What does a routine non-contrast shoulder MRI answer well?
textPreview: What does a routine non-contrast shoulder MRI answer well? | Most primary care questions: cuff tendinosis/tears, subacromial-subdeltoid bursitis, AC joint disease, marrow/occult fracture, and findings supporting adhesive capsulitis.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: For primary care sports medicine, what is the goal when reading a shoulder MRI?
textPreview: For primary care sports medicine, what is the goal when reading a shoulder MRI? | Answer the management question — is there a structural/surgical lesion, a red flag, or a finding that changes the next step (referral, injection, rehab)? — rather than cataloguing every degenerat...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Name three superior-labral 'don't-overcall' variants.
textPreview: Name three superior-labral 'don't-overcall' variants. | Sublabral recess, sublabral foramen, and the Buford complex (absent anterosuperior labrum + a cord-like middle glenohumeral ligament).

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: A spinoglenoid notch paralabral cyst is found — what associated finding must you check?
textPreview: A spinoglenoid notch paralabral cyst is found — what associated finding must you check? | Infraspinatus denervation edema (acute) or fatty atrophy (chronic) from compression of the suprascapular nerve branch.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What is the classic bipolar lesion of an anterior shoulder dislocation?
textPreview: What is the classic bipolar lesion of an anterior shoulder dislocation? | An anteroinferior labral Bankart lesion plus a posterolateral humeral-head Hill-Sachs impaction.

title: Define Bankart, Perthes, ALPSA, and HAGL.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Which instability findings push toward surgery and warrant CT quantification?
textPreview: Which instability findings push toward surgery and warrant CT quantification? | Significant glenoid bone loss (approaching ~20%, with lower subcritical thresholds increasingly used) or an engaging / off-track Hill-Sachs. CT (often 3D) quantifies bone loss best — MRI/MRA may un...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Name shoulder MRI red flags that change management urgently.
textPreview: Name shoulder MRI red flags that change management urgently. | Suspected septic arthritis/osteomyelitis, an aggressive marrow-replacing or soft-tissue mass, and acute occult fracture. Communicate directly and don't anchor on degenerative findings.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What makes a shoulder MRI impression 'management-changing'?
textPreview: What makes a shoulder MRI impression 'management-changing'? | It states the structural diagnosis, its severity/repairability (tendon, size, retraction, muscle quality; bone loss; denervation), and the implied next step (rehab, injection, referral) — rather than a list of every...

title: What are the three MRI grades of a muscle strain, and where do strains most commonly occur?

title: What is the classic mechanism and the most surgically relevant tear location for a pectoralis major rupture?
textPreview: What is the classic mechanism and the most surgically relevant tear location for a pectoralis major rupture? | Eccentric load with the arm extended/abducted — classically the bottom of a bench press. The most surgically relevant location is avulsion of the tendon from its hume...

textPreview: What MRI sign indicates a complete humeral-sided avulsion of the pectoralis major, and what should the report state? | The 'empty bicipital groove' — the avulsed tendon is absent from the lateral lip of the intertubercular groove (partial/MTJ tears retain tendon there). Report...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Reporting a full-thickness cuff tear without muscle quality
textPreview: Always add retraction and muscle atrophy/fatty infiltration because these strongly affect referral urgency and repairability.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Missing denervation because the tendons look intact
textPreview: Inspect muscle edema and fatty change. Suprascapular or axillary nerve patterns can explain weakness with preserved tendon continuity.

title: How to avoid satisfaction-of-search misses
textPreview: ### Satisfaction of Search Shoulder MRI is full of attractive first findings: mild cuff tendinosis, AC arthritis, or a small bursal fluid collection. Those findings may be real but not complete. After the first abnormality, deliberately ask: - Does this fully explain the sympt...

title: Linking MRI findings to management
textPreview: ### Management Translation For primary care sports medicine, useful MRI interpretation includes: - Pain generator likelihood - Tear severity and acuity - Rehab-first versus referral-first features - Injection-relevant findings - Red flags needing escalation Examples: - Mild te...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Common normal variants and pitfalls
textPreview: ### Pitfalls and Variants Common shoulder MRI pitfalls: - Sublabral recess versus SLAP tear - Buford complex versus absent anterosuperior labrum - Magic angle artifact in tendons - Mild bursal fluid overcalled as impingement - Degenerative labral fraying overcalled as traumati...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Tendinosis versus tear
textPreview: ### Tendinosis vs Tear **Tendinosis** is tendon thickening and intermediate signal without a discrete fluid-filled gap or surface defect. **Partial-thickness tears** are subclassified by location: - Articular-sided (deep, joint-facing surface) - Bursal-sided (superficial surfa...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Full-thickness tear size and retraction
textPreview: ### Full-Thickness Tears For full-thickness tears, report: - Tendons involved - AP tear size - Medial retraction: near footprint, humeral head, glenoid, or medial to glenoid - Muscle atrophy and fatty infiltration - Superior humeral head migration - Subacromial-subdeltoid burs...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Subscapularis and biceps pulley relationship
textPreview: ### Subscapularis and Biceps Pulley The upper subscapularis and biceps pulley are a linked system. When one is abnormal, inspect the other. Look for: - Upper-border subscapularis partial tear - Lesser tuberosity footprint involvement - Medial biceps subluxation or dislocation ...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Anterior instability: Bankart spectrum and Hill-Sachs
textPreview: ### Anterior Instability Anterior instability patterns include: - Bankart lesion: detached anteroinferior labrum - Bony Bankart: glenoid rim fracture - Perthes lesion: stripped but minimally displaced labrum - ALPSA: medially displaced labroligamentous complex - Hill-Sachs les...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Bone loss and referral relevance
textPreview: ### Bone Loss Bone loss changes instability management. Estimate and communicate: - Glenoid rim fracture or attritional bone loss - Hill-Sachs size and location - Whether the pattern seems engaging/off-track when advanced assessment is available - Associated cartilage injury P...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Long head biceps tendinosis and tenosynovitis
textPreview: ### Long Head Biceps Evaluate the biceps in three zones: - Anchor at the superior labrum - Intra-articular segment crossing the humeral head - Extra-articular segment in the bicipital groove Look for tendinosis, split tear, partial tear, complete rupture, tenosynovitis, sublux...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Pulley lesions and biceps instability
textPreview: ### Pulley Lesions The biceps pulley includes the superior glenohumeral ligament, coracohumeral ligament, and upper subscapularis/supraspinatus interval. Pulley failure can produce: - Medial biceps subluxation - Biceps dislocation - Upper-border subscapularis tearing - Rotator...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Biceps in the bicipital groove
textPreview: ultrasound: seen: true | appearance: On short-axis the long head of biceps is a hyperechoic oval in the bicipital groove between the tuberosities, ringed by the transverse ligament; a thin anechoic rim of sheath fluid is normal. US is the modality for tenosynovial fluid, tendi...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Subscapularis
textPreview: Don't miss a subscapularis tear — look for biceps medial subluxation/dislocation out of the groove, its companion finding.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Infraspinatus & teres minor
textPreview: Isolated teres-minor fatty atrophy = quadrilateral-space / axillary-nerve problem until proven otherwise.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Glenoid & labrum
textPreview: Trace the labral clock-face; a sublabral foramen (1–3 o'clock) and a Buford complex (absent anterosuperior labrum + cord-like MGHL) are normal variants — don't call them tears. A Buford complex can coexist with and is associated with SLAP pathology, so still inspect the superi...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Humeral head
textPreview: Scan the posterosuperior head for a Hill-Sachs impaction after anterior dislocation; a deep one that engages the glenoid matters.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Anterior labrum
textPreview: Walk the labral clock-face: the anteroinferior labrum (3–6 o'clock) is the Bankart zone. Don't overcall the normal sublabral foramen (1–3 o'clock) or a Buford complex (absent anterosuperior labrum + cord-like middle glenohumeral ligament).

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Biceps in the bicipital groove
textPreview: An empty bicipital groove means the long head is torn or medially dislocated — and medial dislocation implies a torn subscapularis/pulley. Fluid in the groove alone, communicating with the joint, can be normal.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Humeral head
textPreview: variant: Patchy intermediate-signal residual red marrow that follows fat on non-fat-sat sequences is normal — a FOCAL T1-dark area is the worrisome finding (edema, fracture, lesion).

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Anterior labrum
textPreview: variant: Normal anterosuperior variants live around 1-3 o'clock: a sublabral foramen is a smooth anterior-superior cleft, and a Buford complex is absent anterosuperior labrum with a cord-like MGHL. Irregular detachment, periosteal stripping, or bony injury is not a variant.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Biceps in the bicipital groove
textPreview: variant: A thin rim of sheath fluid may communicate with the joint and be normal. The red flags are an empty groove, medial subluxation/dislocation, split tendon, or pulley/subscapularis injury.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Infraspinatus & teres minor
textPreview: The posterior cuff — infraspinatus superior, teres minor inferior. Isolated teres-minor fatty atrophy points to a quadrilateral-space / axillary-nerve problem. | Infraspinatus / teres minor

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Do-not-overcall checkpoint
textPreview: On axial, do the variant check before diagnosing instability: smooth 1-3 o'clock anterosuperior separation may be a sublabral foramen, and absent anterosuperior labrum with a cord-like MGHL is a Buford complex. But do not dismiss an irregular detachment, stripped periosteum, b...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Humeral head
textPreview: The humeral head fills the lateral slices with uniformly BRIGHT fatty marrow. T1 is your marrow sequence — a focal DARK area is the abnormal finding (edema, fracture, or a lesion). | Humeral head

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
itemId: HAGL (humeral avulsion of the glenohumeral ligament)
title: HAGL (humeral avulsion of the glenohumeral ligament)
textPreview: The IGHL torn off the HUMERAL neck (J-sign axillary pouch) — the classic miss when attention is on the glenoid. A surgical instability lesion.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
itemId: Irreparable cuff tear (high Goutallier + narrowed AHI)
title: Irreparable cuff tear (high Goutallier + narrowed AHI)
textPreview: Goutallier ≥3 fatty infiltration with an acromiohumeral interval <7 mm signals a chronic/irreparable tear — changes the surgical conversation.

title: Cam-Type FAI with Anterosuperior Labral Tear and Rim Chondral Injury
textPreview: The alpha angle is measured on oblique-axial (along the neck) or radial images; >55 degrees defines cam morphology. Do not eyeball it on a standard axial - measure it.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Avascular Necrosis of the Femoral Head (Double-Line Sign), Pre-Collapse
textPreview: Use ARCO staging to communicate: stage I (MRI-only changes, e.g. the double-line sign; radiograph normal), II (radiographic sclerosis/cysts, still pre-collapse), III (subchondral crescent/collapse), IV (secondary OA).

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Pincer-Type FAI with Acetabular Retroversion/Over-Coverage and Contrecoup Posteroinferior Chondral Injury
textPreview: Pincer FAI is an ACETABULAR over-coverage/retroversion problem (crossover sign, prominent ischial spine, increased center-edge angle) - the femoral head-neck junction and alpha angle are typically NORMAL, unlike cam.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: An alpha angle >55 degrees defines cam morphology, but measure it on radial reformats around the head-neck junction (maximal between 1 and 2 o'clock) — a single straight axial slice misses the anterosuperior bump and underestimates the deformity.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: A lateral center-edge angle <20 degrees indicates dysplasia (undercoverage, instability risk) and >40 degrees indicates pincer over-coverage; the identical labral tear is managed very differently depending on which side of that range the hip sits.

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine

courseTitle: Hip MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: Ischiofemoral impingement is suggested by quadratus femoris edema with narrowing of both the ischiofemoral space (<17 mm) and the quadratus femoris space (<8 mm); it is an under-recognized cause of deep buttock pain that mimics hamstring or sciatic pathology.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Degenerative Knee — Tricompartmental Osteoarthritis
textPreview: Meniscal extrusion >3mm is a marker of root tear or severe degeneration and correlates with accelerated OA progression.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Medial Meniscal Root Tear
textPreview: Meniscal extrusion >3mm on coronal images is a secondary sign of root tear.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
textPreview: Meniscal extrusion >3 mm is abnormal and should trigger a careful search for root or radial tears and degeneration; extrusion alone does not diagnose a root tear. Trace the root on coronal and axial images.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the magic angle artifact?
textPreview: What is the magic angle artifact? | The magic angle artifact occurs when a structure is oriented at approximately 55 degrees to the main magnetic field (B0). At this angle, the apparent T2 relaxation time increases, causing the structure to appear falsely bright on short TE se...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the quadriceps tendon?
textPreview: What is the quadriceps tendon? | The quadriceps tendon is a trilaminar structure inserting on the superior pole of the patella, approximately 8-10 mm thick. Its three layers correspond to the rectus femoris (superficial), vastus lateralis/medialis (middle), and vastus intermed...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is the two-slice-touch rule?
textPreview: What is the two-slice-touch rule? | The two-slice-touch rule is a high-specificity confidence rule: surface-reaching meniscal signal on at least two consecutive standard 3-4 mm images has a much higher positive predictive value than contact on one image. It reduces false posit...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What is meniscal extrusion and how do I measure it?
textPreview: What is meniscal extrusion and how do I measure it? | Meniscal extrusion is the displacement of the meniscal body beyond the tibial margin, measured on coronal images at the mid-body level. Greater than 3 mm is clinically significant and should prompt a deliberate search for a...

title: How do I grade cartilage damage?
textPreview: How do I grade cartilage damage? | Cartilage is graded by modified Outerbridge and by ICRS — they diverge at the deepest lesions, so name the system. Grade 0 normal; 1 signal change, intact surface; 2 defect <50% depth; 3 defect >50% depth (ICRS 3 also includes full-thickness ...

title: What is magic angle artifact, and how do you confirm it is not a true meniscal tear?
textPreview: What is magic angle artifact, and how do you confirm it is not a true meniscal tear? | Magic angle artifact causes increased signal in structures oriented at 55 degrees to the main magnetic field on short TE sequences (T1, PD). It disappears on T2. If meniscal signal on PD dis...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: What are the two layers of the MCL and what structure does the deep layer attach to?
textPreview: What are the two layers of the MCL and what structure does the deep layer attach to? | Superficial MCL (primary valgus stabilizer, extends 5-7 cm below the joint line) and deep MCL (meniscofemoral and meniscotibial ligaments that attach directly to the medial meniscus).

title: What is the two-slice-touch rule?
textPreview: What is the two-slice-touch rule? | Surface-reaching meniscal signal on at least two consecutive standard 3-4 mm images gives high confidence for a tear and reduces false positives from volume averaging and pseudotears. It is a confidence rule, not an absolute gate: a one-imag...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: How is meniscal extrusion measured, and what threshold is pathologic?
textPreview: How is meniscal extrusion measured, and what threshold is pathologic? | Measured on coronal images at the meniscal mid-body: distance from the outer tibial plateau margin to the outermost meniscal edge. Greater than 3 mm beyond the tibial plateau margin is the conventional pat...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: At what meniscal extrusion threshold should you actively search for a meniscal root tear?
textPreview: At what meniscal extrusion threshold should you actively search for a meniscal root tear? | Extrusion >3 mm is pathologic and should prompt an active search for a meniscal root tear on coronal and axial images; report the measured value rather than assigning a separate ">5 mm ...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Where is the pes anserine bursa located and what clinical condition does pes anserine bursitis mimic?
textPreview: Where is the pes anserine bursa located and what clinical condition does pes anserine bursitis mimic? | Located approximately 4-5 cm below the medial joint line, deep to the sartorius/gracilis/semitendinosus tendons. It commonly mimics medial meniscal tear or MCL injury clinic...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Not checking the meniscal root when you see extrusion >3mm
textPreview: Extrusion >3mm is pathologic and should trigger a careful root-tear search, especially when there is medial compartment overload or SIFK. Trace the root on coronal and axial images.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Menisci — the wedges
textPreview: Measure medial extrusion at the mid-body — >3 mm beyond the tibial margin is significant and should prompt a deliberate root/cartilage search; extrusion alone is not a root tear.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Anterior cruciate ligament
textPreview: variant: A thick, bulbous ACL with increased signal but CONTINUOUS, parallel 'celery-stalk' fibers and no secondary signs is mucoid degeneration, not a tear — don't operate. | measure: Quantify the secondary signs of a tear: anterior tibial translation >5 mm (lateral sagittal ...

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Medial collateral ligament
textPreview: measure: The superficial MCL runs from the medial femoral epicondyle to ~5–7 cm below the joint line on the tibia — trace its full length on coronal before calling it intact.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Meniscus — the dark bow-ties
textPreview: measure: Three or more contiguous bow-tie slices (4–5 mm sagittal) — or a central coronal body width >13–15 mm — is a discoid meniscus, not normal.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: MPFL / medial retinaculum
textPreview: measure: Screen for predisposing anatomy: patella alta (Insall-Salvati >1.2) and elevated TT-TG. The classic >20 mm TT-TG cutoff is a risk marker, but MRI technique and landmarks can shift the value by a few millimeters.

courseTitle: UCLA Knee MRI Interpretation Course
title: Menisci — the wedges
textPreview: Each meniscus is a low-signal triangle pointing into the joint. On coronal, check that the medial body doesn't extrude past the tibial margin — >3 mm is significant, but it is a clue to inspect the posterior root rather than a root-tear diagnosis by itself. Laterally, do not m...

textPreview: Trace each meniscus from anterior horn through body to posterior horn, evaluate roots, apply the two-slice-touch rule, and measure extrusion. | Evaluate medial meniscus: anterior horn, body, posterior horn | Evaluate lateral meniscus: anterior horn, body, posterior horn | Chec...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: Goutallier/Fuchs fatty atrophy is a key reparability marker: grade 3-4 (fat equal to or exceeding muscle) on the sagittal Y-view is associated with lower reparability and higher re-tear risk. Weigh it with tear size, retraction, chronicity, and patient factors, and always comm...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: The supraspinatus 'critical zone,' about 1 cm proximal to the footprint, is hypovascular and the most common site of degenerative tears. Scrutinize it on every fluid-sensitive coronal and sagittal image.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: On a fluid-sensitive sagittal at the level of the coracoid base (the 'Y' or Mercedes view), the supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, and subscapularis are all profiled — the standard plane for grading Goutallier fatty atrophy and muscle bulk.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: A normal sublabral recess is a smooth, medially-oriented cleft at the 11-1 o'clock position that follows the glenoid contour; a SLAP tear extends laterally/posteriorly, is irregular and wide (>2-3 mm), and tracks fluid or contrast into the labral substance.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: Adhesive capsulitis is a soft-tissue MR diagnosis: thickening and edema of the axillary recess capsule (>4 mm), obliteration of the rotator-interval fat by enhancing soft tissue, and thickening of the coracohumeral ligament. The cuff itself is typically normal.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
textPreview: Massive retracted cuff tears with a high-riding humerus (acromiohumeral interval <7 mm) and grade 3-4 fatty atrophy define cuff-tear arthropathy; these are generally irreparable and steer management toward reverse total shoulder arthroplasty rather than repair.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Which plane best shows the subscapularis, the biceps in/out of its groove, the labrum (Bankart), and Hill-Sachs?
textPreview: Which plane best shows the subscapularis, the biceps in/out of its groove, the labrum (Bankart), and Hill-Sachs? | Axial. The oblique sagittal is best for muscle bulk/fatty infiltration (Goutallier 'Y' view), which tendon is torn, the rotator interval, and acromial morphology.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: How is partial-tear depth graded, and why does it matter?
textPreview: How is partial-tear depth graded, and why does it matter? | Low-grade <50% vs high-grade >50% of tendon thickness. A high-grade partial behaves clinically closer to a significant structural lesion — not 'just tendinosis.'

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What five things should every full-thickness cuff tear report include?
textPreview: What five things should every full-thickness cuff tear report include? | (1) which tendon(s); (2) surface/thickness; (3) AP tear size; (4) retraction (Patte stage); (5) muscle atrophy / fatty infiltration (Goutallier grade). Surface and muscle quality are what change management.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What Goutallier grade predicts poor healing / an irreparable tear, and where is it assessed?
textPreview: What Goutallier grade predicts poor healing / an irreparable tear, and where is it assessed? | Grade ≥3 (fat ≥ muscle). Assess on the oblique-sagittal 'Y' view at the scapular spine.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: What acromiohumeral interval values signal cuff insufficiency and cuff-tear arthropathy?
textPreview: What acromiohumeral interval values signal cuff insufficiency and cuff-tear arthropathy? | Normal >7 mm. <7 mm suggests cuff insufficiency with superior humeral migration. <5 mm is consistent with a chronic massive tear / cuff-tear arthropathy.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: You see the supraspinatus tendon on the coronal. Find its MUSCLE belly on the sagittal T1 (the Goutallier slice).
textPreview: You see the supraspinatus tendon on the coronal. Find its MUSCLE belly on the sagittal T1 (the Goutallier slice). | On the scapular 'Y' the supraspinatus sits above the spine; T1 is the sequence to grade its fatty atrophy (Goutallier), which predicts repairability. | plane: Co...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: This is the infraspinatus on the sagittal T2-FS (posterior cuff). Find the same muscle on the sagittal T1 Goutallier slice -- don't grab teres minor below it.
textPreview: This is the infraspinatus on the sagittal T2-FS (posterior cuff). Find the same muscle on the sagittal T1 Goutallier slice -- don't grab teres minor below it. | T1 is the fatty-atrophy slice that grades Goutallier and predicts repairability. Infraspinatus fills the infraspinou...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: This is the supraspinatus tendon on the coronal T2-FS. Find the same tendon on the sagittal-Y, arcing over the humeral head beneath the acromial outlet.
textPreview: This is the supraspinatus tendon on the coronal T2-FS. Find the same tendon on the sagittal-Y, arcing over the humeral head beneath the acromial outlet. | Coronal measures gap and retraction; the sag-Y shows WHICH cuff tendons are involved -- the board answer for extension. Mo...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Rotator-cuff muscles (medial slice)
textPreview: ultrasound: seen: true | appearance: US shows the cuff muscle bellies as hypoechoic muscle with hyperechoic perimysial striations, and can grossly suggest atrophy by bulk loss and increased echogenicity (fatty change). But Goutallier fatty-grading and the deep supraspinatus-fo...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Subacromial–subdeltoid bursa
textPreview: ultrasound: seen: true | appearance: A thin hypo/anechoic stripe with hyperechoic peribursal fat sandwiched between deltoid and the cuff — US is the modality of choice. Distension (>2 mm fluid), wall thickening, or bunching/catching under the acromion on dynamic abduction sign...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Rotator-cuff muscles (medial slice)
textPreview: Grade fatty atrophy on this sagittal view (Goutallier–Fuchs) — it predicts whether a cuff tear is repairable.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Supraspinatus tendon
textPreview: Scrutinize the 'critical zone' ~1 cm from the footprint, the articular surface for a PASTA tear, and whether the rotator cable is intact.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Bony glenoid
textPreview: Read the bony rim on the axial — an anteroinferior bony Bankart (osseous fragment or blunted rim) changes management; substantial bone loss (often ~20–25%, with lower subcritical ranges relevant in contact athletes) supports considering bony augmentation such as Latarjet, espe...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Coracoid process
textPreview: The coracoid anchors the conjoint tendon and the coracoclavicular ligaments; a short coracohumeral distance (<6 mm) can support subcoracoid impingement of the subscapularis in the right clinical/imaging context.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Rotator-cuff muscles (medial slice)
textPreview: measure: Grade fatty infiltration on the oblique-sagittal 'Y' at the scapular spine (Goutallier 0–4); grade ≥3 = fat ≥ muscle.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Supraspinatus tendon
textPreview: variant: Distal-supraspinatus intermediate signal that vanishes on T2 is magic angle (tendon curving ~55° to B0 on short-TE T1/PD), not a tear. | measure: Partial tear is low-grade <50% vs high-grade >50% of thickness; stage retraction by Patte (1 = footprint, 2 = humeral head...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Biceps & rotator interval
textPreview: variant: Mild interval signal or fluid is nonspecific. A pulley lesion needs loss/defect of the SGHL/CHL sling, medial biceps subluxation, or a matching upper-subscapularis tear pattern. | measure: In adhesive capsulitis, rotator-interval capsule thickness ≥7 mm and coracohume...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Greater tuberosity
textPreview: measure: Acromiohumeral interval ≤7 mm supports chronic cuff insufficiency/superior migration, especially on radiographs; MRI-based AHI is supportive but not interchangeable with a properly positioned radiographic measurement.

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Axillary recess
textPreview: variant: Prominent synovial folds in the U-shaped pouch can mimic loose bodies; trace them as attached folds across slices before calling a free body. | measure: Routine MRI axillary-pouch/IGHL thickness >4 mm supports adhesive capsulitis; MRA thresholds near >3 mm have been u...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Rotator-cuff muscles (medial slice)
textPreview: On the medial sagittal slices the four cuff muscle BELLIES are seen in cross-section around the scapula — supraspinatus on top (in the fossa above the spine), subscapularis anterior, infraspinatus and teres minor posterior. This is THE view to grade muscle bulk and fatty atrop...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Supraspinatus tendon
textPreview: Followed along its length to the footprint — uniform low signal. Watch the hypovascular 'critical zone' ~1 cm from the footprint, where degenerative tears begin; a bright fluid gap = a tear. Also recognize the cable/crescent model: the thicker rotator cable sits just medial to...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
title: Rotator-cuff muscles (medial slice)
textPreview: The scapular 'Y' shows all four cuff bellies at once — supraspinatus above the spine, infraspinatus below it, subscapularis anterior, teres minor posteroinferior. This is THE slice to grade fatty atrophy (Goutallier–Fuchs): on T1, fatty streaks brighten within a wasted muscle,...

courseTitle: Shoulder MRI for Primary Care Sports Medicine
itemId: Critical glenoid bone loss / off-track Hill-Sachs
title: Critical glenoid bone loss / off-track Hill-Sachs
textPreview: ≥20–25% glenoid loss or an engaging Hill-Sachs shifts a soft-tissue Bankart repair toward a bony procedure (Latarjet). CT quantifies best.
